TL;DR: The paper critiques the method of ranking large language models (LLMs) via human feedback, showing that global rankings are misleading due to heterogeneity in opinions across languages, tasks, and time. They propose a novel framework to better represent diverse user preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for ranking LLMs based on human feedback via global Bradley-Terry rankings fail to account for significant opinion diversity across demographics, which limits their reliability and application.

Method: The authors introduce $(λ, ν)$-portfolios, a model selection framework formulated as a set cover problem, supported by VC dimension guarantees, to better align rankings with user populations, addressing opinion heterogeneity.

Result: Their method produces highly consistent rankings by grouping votes by language and user subpopulations, achieving better coverage and predictability than global rankings. On Arena data, they recover 5 distinct rankings covering 96% of votes compared to 21% coverage via global ranking.

Conclusion: Global rankings for LLMs are inadequate due to opinion heterogeneity, and the proposed $(λ, ν)$-portfolio framework provides a more effective method to rank models across diverse user groups, offering applications in fairness and policy analysis.

Abstract: Ranking LLMs via pairwise human feedback underpins current leaderboards for open-ended tasks, such as creative writing and problem-solving. We analyze ~89K comparisons in 116 languages from 52 LLMs from Arena, and show that the best-fit global Bradley-Terry (BT) ranking is misleading. Nearly 2/3 of the decisive votes cancel out, and even the top 50 models according to the global BT ranking are statistically indistinguishable (pairwise win probabilities are at most 0.53 within the top 50 models). We trace this failure to strong, structured heterogeneity of opinions across language, task, and time. Moreover, we find an important characteristic - *language* plays a key role. Grouping by language (and families) increases the agreement of votes massively, resulting in two orders of magnitude higher spread in the ELO scores (i.e., very consistent rankings). What appears as global noise is in fact a mixture of coherent but conflicting subpopulations.
  To address such heterogeneity in supervised machine learning, we introduce the framework of $(λ, ν)$-portfolios, which are small sets of models that achieve a prediction error at most $λ$, "covering" at least a $ν$ fraction of users. We formulate this as a variant of the set cover problem and provide guarantees using the VC dimension of the underlying set system. On the Arena data, our algorithms recover just 5 distinct BT rankings that cover over 96% of votes at a modest $λ$, compared to the 21% coverage by the global ranking. We also provide a portfolio of 6 LLMs that cover twice as many votes as the top-6 LLMs from a global ranking. We further construct portfolios for a classification problem on the COMPAS dataset using an ensemble of fairness-regularized classification models and show that these portfolios can be used to detect blind spots in the data, which might be of independent interest to policymakers.

</details>


### [582] [UniPool: A Globally Shared Expert Pool for Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06665)
*Minbin Huang,Han Shi,Chuanyang Zheng,Yimeng Wu,Guoxuan Chen,Xintong Yu,Yichun Yin,Hong Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UniPool, a novel MoE architecture sharing a global expert pool across layers rather than using layer-specific experts, showing improved validation loss and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in conventional MoE architectures which allocate expert capacity per layer, the authors explore the redundancy and propose a shared global pool architecture.

Method: UniPool replaces layer-specific experts with a shared expert pool accessed by independent routers and adds mechanisms for stable and balanced training, including a pool-level auxiliary loss and NormRouter for sparse routing.

Result: UniPool outperformed conventional MoE architectures in validation loss and perplexity across multiple model scales, while using expert parameters more efficiently (even with reduced budgets).

Conclusion: UniPool demonstrates that expert capacity can scale sublinearly with depth under a shared-pool design, making it more efficient and effective compared to traditional MoE approaches.

Abstract: Modern Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures allocate expert capacity through a rigid per-layer rule: each transformer layer owns a separate expert set. This convention couples depth scaling with linear expert-parameter growth and assumes that every layer needs isolated expert capacity. However, recent analyses and our routing probe challenge this allocation rule: replacing a deeper layer's learned top-k router with uniform random routing drops downstream accuracy by only 1.0-1.6 points across multiple production MoE models. Motivated by this redundancy, we propose UniPool, an MoE architecture that treats expert capacity as a global architectural budget by replacing per-layer expert ownership with a single shared pool accessed by independent per-layer routers. To enable stable and balanced training under sharing, we introduce a pool-level auxiliary loss that balances expert utilization across the entire pool, and adopt NormRouter to provide sparse and scale-stable routing into the shared expert pool. Across five LLaMA-architecture model scales (182M, 469M, 650M, 830M, and 978M parameters) trained on 30B tokens from the Pile, UniPool consistently improves validation loss and perplexity over the matched vanilla MoE baselines. Across these scales, UniPool reduces validation loss by up to 0.0386 relative to vanilla MoE. Beyond raw loss improvement, our results identify pool size as an explicit depth-scaling hyperparameter: reduced-pool UniPool variants using only 41.6%-66.7% of the vanilla expert-parameter budget match or outperform layer-wise MoE at the tested scales. This shows that, under a shared-pool design, expert parameters need not grow linearly with depth; they can grow sublinearly while remaining more efficient and effective than vanilla MoE. Further analysis shows that UniPool's benefits compose with finer-grained expert decomposition.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [583] [Direct From Darwin: Deriving Advanced Optimizers From Evolutionary First Principles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05284)
*Daniel Grimmer*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Darwinian Lineage Simulations (DLS) to reconcile Fisher's and Wright's opposing views on evolution by unifying deterministic and stochastic perspectives. It proves that gradient-based optimization algorithms like SGD and Adam can simulate evolutionary dynamics after incorporating structured noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between high-performance optimization tools and rigorous simulations of Darwinian evolution, addressing the lack of evolutionary fidelity in modern approaches.

Method: The paper derives a suite of optimization algorithms grounded in evolutionary first principles and introduces DLS to unify deterministic (Fisher) and stochastic (Wright) models of evolution using structured noise.

Result: By introducing DLS noise, traditional optimization algorithms such as SGD and Adam are adapted to realistically simulate evolutionary processes, achieving compatibility between optimization and evolution.

Conclusion: The study provides a robust framework demonstrating that common optimization algorithms can be transformed into scientifically valid simulations of evolution, enhancing evolutionary biology applications.

Abstract: Evolutionary computation has long promised to deliver both high-performance optimization tools as well as rigorous scientific simulations of Darwinian evolution. However, modern algorithms frequently abandon evolutionary fidelity for physics-inspired heuristics or superficial biological metaphors. This paper derives a suite of advanced gradient-based optimization algorithms directly from evolutionary first principles. We introduce Darwinian Lineage Simulations (DLS) to prove that, in an asexual context, Fisher's and Wright's historically opposed views of evolution are actually formally equivalent. This unification requires carefully partitioning Fisher's deterministically-evolving total population into Wright's randomly-drifting sub-populations. We prove that proper bookkeeping requires introducing a specific kind of structured noise (the DLS noise relation). Crucially, however, any bookkeeping choices which satisfy this relation will result in a faithful simulation of evolution. Using this vast representational freedom, we prove that a broad family of battle-tested optimization algorithms are already perfectly compatible with evolutionary dynamics. These include: Stochastic Gradient Descent, Natural Gradient Descent, and the Damped Newton's method among many others. By simply adding DLS noise (i.e., evolutionarily faithful genetic drift), these algorithms become scientifically valid in silico simulations of Darwinian evolution. Finally, we demonstrate that even the state-of-the-art Adam optimizer can be brought into evolutionary compliance through a minor mathematical surgery.

</details>


### [584] [Efficient event-driven retrieval in high-capacity kernel Hopfield networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05978)
*Akira Tamamori*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper explores asynchronous retrieval dynamics in Kernel Logistic Regression (KLR) Hopfield networks, demonstrating that asynchronous updates can achieve similar recall accuracy and trajectories as synchronous updates while supporting high storage capacities and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the computational inefficiency of synchronous updates in high-capacity associative memory models like KLR Hopfield networks, particularly for deployment on energy-efficient, event-driven neuromorphic hardware.

Method: The authors analyze asynchronous sequential updates in KLR Hopfield networks, testing recall accuracy, storage capacities, and computational efficiency through empirical evaluations of random patterns and the number of state transitions required for error correction.

Result: The results reveal that asynchronous updates statistically replicate synchronous dynamics, achieve high recall accuracy, and support empirical storage capacities up to $P/N ≈ 30$ in random pattern regimes. Computational efficiency is achieved with convergence close to the initial Hamming distance without causing spurious oscillations.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that KLR learning creates a smooth energy landscape conducive to efficient, event-driven computation, enabling scalable, low-power associative memory systems suitable for neuromorphic architectures.

Abstract: High-capacity associative memory models, such as Kernel Logistic Regression (KLR) Hopfield networks, have demonstrated strong storage capabilities but typically rely on computationally expensive synchronous updates. This reliance poses a bottleneck for deployment on energy-efficient, event-driven neuromorphic hardware. In this paper, we investigate the asynchronous retrieval dynamics of KLR Hopfield networks. We show empirically that, under appropriately tuned kernel parameters, asynchronous sequential updates exhibit trajectories that are statistically indistinguishable from those of synchronous dynamics, while maintaining high recall accuracy within the tested regime for random patterns. Furthermore, we find that the asynchronous network achieves empirical storage capacities approaching $P/N \approx 30$ in static random pattern regimes, exceeding classical limits. To evaluate computational efficiency, we analyze the total number of state transitions (bit flips) required for error correction. The results show that the network converges using a number of events close to the initial Hamming distance from the target pattern, without observable spurious oscillations. These findings suggest that the large-margin attractors induced by KLR learning create a smooth energy landscape suited for sparse, event-driven computation, providing a basis for scalable and low-power associative memory on neuromorphic architectures.

</details>


### [585] [CoupleEvo: Evolving Heuristics for Coupled Optimization Problems Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06341)
*Thomas Bömer,Bastian Amberg,Max Disselnmeyer,Anne Meyer*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper proposes CoupleEvo, a method for evolving heuristics to solve coupled optimization problems using three evolutionary strategies: sequential, iterative, and integrated. Experimental results suggest decomposition-based strategies outperform the integrated one.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of current LLM-driven heuristic design approaches that focus only on single-problem settings by creating a method applicable to complex, coupled optimization problems.

Method: Developed and compared three evolutionary coordination strategies – sequential, iterative, and integrated – to evolve heuristics for coupled optimization problems. These were evaluated experimentally on two representative problems.

Result: Decomposition-based strategies (sequential and iterative) showed more stable convergence and higher solution quality compared to the integrated strategy, which had higher complexity and variability.

Conclusion: Coordinating the evolutionary search process across interdependent subproblems is crucial for improving solution quality, and LLM-driven heuristic design holds significant potential for addressing coupled optimization problems.

Abstract: Many real-world optimization problems consist of multiple tightly coupled subproblems whose solutions must be coordinated to achieve high overall performance. However, existing large language model driven automated heuristic design approaches are limited to single-problem settings. In this paper, we propose CoupleEvo. CoupleEvo proposes three evolutionary coordination strategies to evolve heuristics for coupled optimization problems: the sequential strategy evolves heuristics for one subproblem after the other; the iterative strategy alternates the evolution of heuristics for different subproblems over successive generations; and the integrated strategy evolves heuristics for all problems simultaneously. The approach is evaluated on two representative coupled optimization problems. Experimental results show that decomposition-based strategies (sequential and iterative) provide more stable convergence and higher solution quality, while the integrated evolution strategy suffers from increased search complexity and variability. These findings highlight the importance of coordinating evolutionary search across interdependent subproblems and demonstrate the potential of LLM-driven heuristic design for complex coupled optimization problems. The code is available: https://github.com/tb-git-kit-research/CoupleEvo.

</details>


<div id='cs.PF'></div>

# cs.PF [[Back]](#toc)

### [586] [When Quantization Is Free: An int4 KV Cache That Outruns fp16 on Apple Silicon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05699)
*Mohamed Amine Bergach*

Main category: cs.PF

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fused KV-cache quantization kernel on Apple Silicon's unified memory that operates faster than fp16 for specific token prefixes while maintaining model quality with reduced memory usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a faster and more memory-efficient KV-cache quantization method on Apple Silicon hardware, particularly addressing the trade-off between speed, memory, and quality.

Method: A single fused Metal kernel incorporates steps like sign-randomized FFT, per-channel scaling, per-group abs-max, and int4 nibble packing. It is implemented as a HuggingFace Cache subclass to compress memory while preserving computational efficiency.

Result: The kernel outperformed fp16 in processing speed (-0.7% to -8% ms/tok) across various models and prefixes, achieved 3x memory compression, and reduced perplexity degradation for both standard and catastrophic cases.

Conclusion: The fused kernel effectively combines quantization and computational steps to optimize speed, memory usage, and quality, thereby making it highly suitable for Apple Silicon environments.

Abstract: KV-cache quantization is framed as a quality--latency trade-off. We show it is \emph{inverted} on Apple Silicon's unified memory: a single fused Metal kernel (sign-randomized FFT $+$ per-channel $λ$ $+$ per-group abs-max $+$ int4 nibble pack), exposed as a HuggingFace \texttt{Cache} subclass, runs \emph{faster than fp16} across $256$--$4096$-token prefixes on Gemma-3 1B ($-3$ to $-8\%$ ms/tok) and at short context on Qwen2.5-1.5B ($-0.7$ to $-2.6\%$ through $1$K), with $3\times$ persistent memory compression and quality preserved ($\dPPL = 0.000$ Qwen short-prompt; $+3.6$ hook $\dPPL$ Gemma). The kernel's $\sim\!25$\,ns/vec overhead is below the bandwidth savings from $3\times$ compression. The fused kernel also closes Qwen's 4-bit per-token catastrophe ($\dPPL = +7975 \to +638.6$, $12.5\times$ reduction) at $182$\,GFLOPS / $D{=}128$. Supporting findings: $\SRFT$ and $\SRHT$ are statistically indistinguishable for KV quality (we pick $\SRFT$ for mixed-radix and matrix-multiply alignment); a learned-rotation ablation surfaces a regularization role for the fixed random SRFT base (learning $R+λ$ without SRFT lowers calibration MSE $84.9\%$ vs $50.3\%$ but yields worse PPL); Householder rotations at $k{=}d/2$ reflectors are effectively lossless at $d{=}256$.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [587] [Governed Metaprogramming for Intelligent Systems: Reclassifying Eval as a Governed Effec](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05248)
*Alan L. McCann*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: This paper proposes a language design, governed metaprogramming, where the transition from machine form to executable authority is treated as a governed effect subject to structural checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional programming languages treat eval as an unrestricted primitive for executing generated code, leading to potential security and compliance concerns in complex intelligent systems.

Method: The paper introduces governed metaprogramming, a language framework where code manipulation remains pure, and execution (materialization) is inspected for compliance, resource use, and other policies before authorization.

Result: The authors formalize evaluation and materialization processes, prove properties (e.g., purity and boundary preservation), and implement the framework in a domain-specific language (DSL) called MashinTalk, validating it using existing machine-checked theorems.

Conclusion: Governed metaprogramming reclassifies eval as a controlled effect, offering a more secure and manageable approach to executing generated code in intelligent systems.

Abstract: AI systems increasingly synthesize executable structure at runtime: LLMs generate programs, agents construct workflows,self-improving systems modify their own behavior. In classical homoiconic and staged languages, the transition from coderepresentation to execution is unrestricted. eval is a language primitive, not a governed operation. We argue that ingovernedintelligent systems, this transition is an authority amplification: it converts symbolic structure into executableauthority andmust be mediated like any other effect. We present governed metaprogramming, a language design where programrepresentations(machine forms) are first-class values, form manipulation is pure computation, and materialization (the transition fromform toexecutable machine) is a governed effect subject to structural inspection. The governance system analyzes the proposedprogram'scapability requirements, policy compliance, and resource estimates before permitting execution. We formalize twojudgments: pureform evaluation (which emits no directives) and governed materialization (which emits exactly one governed directive). Weprovethree properties: purity of form manipulation, the no-bypass theorem, and boundary preservation. We implement the designinMashinTalk, a DSL for AI workflows compiling to BEAM bytecode, and report on integration with 454 existingmachine-checked Rocqtheorems. The central contribution is reclassifying eval from a language primitive into a governed effect.

</details>


### [588] [Beyond BLEU: A Semantic Evaluation Method for Code Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05282)
*Julius Näumann,Sven Keidel,Amir Molzam Sharifloo,Mira Mezini*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper introduces an evaluation methodology prioritizing semantic equivalence over syntax for code translation tasks, leveraging compiler-based testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Common metrics like BLEU fail to capture semantic correctness in code translations, creating a gap in meaningful evaluation methods.

Method: The authors apply compiler testing methodologies to evaluate binary lifting tasks, introducing a semantic correctness score as a metric.

Result: LLM-based decompilers outperform heuristic methods in terms of semantic correctness, while BLEU scores poorly correlate with functional accuracy.

Conclusion: Semantic evaluations are essential, as syntactic metrics like BLEU are inadequate for assessing the functional accuracy of code translations.

Abstract: Code translation is one of the core capabilities of LLMs. However, evaluating the correctness of translations remains difficult, as commonly used metrics such as BLEU measure only syntactic similarity, disregarding program semantics. We propose a novel evaluation methodology for code translation tasks, emphasizing semantic equivalence over surface-level string similarity. Our approach applies established compiler testing methodology to a new domain, allowing the assessment of an LLM fine-tuned for binary lifting tasks (i.e. decompiling binaries to higher-level representations). We introduce a semantic correctness score, defined as the proportion of translations that produce correct execution outcomes, and demonstrate its application by evaluating LLM-based and heuristic decompilers. Our findings show that LLM-based approaches significantly outperform heuristic ones, while BLEU scores show negligible correlation with semantic correctness (r = -0.127 to 0.354), demonstrating that syntactic metrics fail to predict functional accuracy.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [589] [A GPU-Accelerated Hybrid Method for a Class of Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05208)
*Zhenyu Lei,Jin-Kao Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid algorithm is proposed for solving multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRPs), leveraging advanced search techniques and GPU acceleration for improved performance, especially on large-scale instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDVRPs are computationally difficult due to their complexity, and solving these problems efficiently has significant practical applications.

Method: The algorithm integrates a route-exchange crossover, a feasible-and-infeasible search framework, dedicated local search operators, tensor-based GPU acceleration, and a multi-move update strategy.

Result: The proposed algorithms demonstrated strong competitiveness with state-of-the-art methods in benchmark evaluations for three MDVRP variants, particularly excelling in handling large-scale problems.

Conclusion: The hybrid algorithm effectively addresses the complexities of MDVRPs with significant improvements in performance and scalability over existing methods.

Abstract: Multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRPs) are prevalent in a variety of practical applications. However, they are computationally challenging to solve due to their inherent complexity. This paper proposes an effective hybrid algorithm for a class of MDVRPs. The algorithm integrates a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover and a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search framework guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function. Two dedicated depot-related local search operators are incorporated to further strengthen the search capability in multi-depot settings. To improve computational efficiency and scalability, an enhanced version of the algorithm is developed that uses a tensor-based GPU acceleration combined with a novel multi-move update strategy. Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show that the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, especially for large-scale instances.

</details>


### [590] [Topology-Driven Anti-Entanglement Control for Soft Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05236)
*Haoyang Le,Shengxuan Wang,Mohan Chen,Shuo Feng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a topology-driven framework (TD-MARL) for enhancing anti-winding coordination among soft robots in complex environments, outperforming existing DRL methods in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Navigating highly constrained environments while avoiding entanglement remains a challenge for multi-robot systems, necessitating improved coordination strategies.

Method: The TD-MARL framework employs centralized learning for state sharing and distributed execution for robust performance, with an added topological security layer to mitigate entanglement risks.

Result: Experiments demonstrate improved convergence rates and anti-winding performance compared to current DRL approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed TD-MARL framework effectively addresses limitations in existing distributed training, enhancing multi-robot collaboration in precision manufacturing environments.

Abstract: In the field of precision manufacturing in complex constrained environments, the role of soft robots is increasingly prominent, and the realization of anti-winding control based on multi-intelligent body reinforcement learning has become a research hotspot. One of the core problems at present is to coordinate multiple robots to complete the unwinding operation in a highly constrained environment. The existing distributed training framework faces some observability challenges in high-density barrier and unstable environments, resulting in poor learning results. This paper proposes a topology-driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (TD-MARL) framework to coordinate multi-robot systems to avoid entanglement. Specifically, the critical network adopts centralized learning, so that each intelligent body can perceive the strategies of other intelligent bodies by sharing the topological state, thus alleviating the training instability caused by complex interactions; eliminating the demand for communication resources between robots through distributed execution, Upgrade system reliability; the integrated topological security layer uses topological invariants to accurately assess and mitigate the risk of entanglement to avoid the strategy from falling into local difficulties. Finally, the full simulation experiments carried out in the real simulation environment show that the method is better than the current advanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method in terms of convergence and anti-winding effect.

</details>


### [591] [DexSim2Real: Foundation Model-Guided Sim-to-Real Transfer for Generalizable Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05241)
*Zijian Zeng,Fei Ding,Huiming Yang,Xianwei Li,Yuhao Liao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSim2Real is a novel framework that uses vision-language foundation models to address the sim-to-real gap in dexterous robot manipulation tasks, achieving a high real-world success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The critical bottleneck in deploying simulated dexterous manipulation policies to real-world robots is the lack of generalizability in current techniques relying on manual domain randomization or task-specific adaptation.

Method: DexSim2Real incorporates three components: FM-DR using vision-language feedback for simulation realism, TVCAP for visuo-tactile policy adaptation, and PSC for progressive skill training based on difficulty scheduling.

Result: Tests on six manipulation tasks show DexSim2Real achieves 78.2% real-world success rate and reduces the sim-to-real gap to 8.3%, outperforming existing methods like DrEureka and DeXtreme.

Conclusion: DexSim2Real provides a generalized solution for sim-to-real transfer in dexterous manipulation, leveraging advanced vision-language models and structured skill training to significantly improve real-world performance.

Abstract: Sim-to-real transfer remains a critical bottleneck for deploying dexterous manipulation policies learned in simulation to real-world robots. Existing approaches rely on manually designed domain randomization or task-specific adaptation, limiting their generalizability across diverse manipulation scenarios. We present DexSim2Real, an integrated framework that leverages vision-language foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap for dexterous manipulation. Our system combines three components: (1) Foundation Model-Guided Domain Randomization (FM-DR), which uses a vision-language model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches like DrEureka with direct visual feedback; (2) a Tactile-Visual Cross-Attention Policy (TVCAP) that adapts cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion to zero-shot sim-to-real RL; and (3) a Progressive Skill Curriculum (PSC) that builds on LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks. Extensive experiments on six challenging manipulation tasks with blinded evaluation demonstrate that DexSim2Real achieves a 78.2% average real-world success rate, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme while reducing the sim-to-real performance gap to only 8.3%.

</details>


### [592] [Track A*: Fast Visibility-Aware Trajectory Planning for Active Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05338)
*Hanxuan Chen,Kangli Wang,Ji Pei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Track A star" (TA star), an offline trajectory planner optimized for visibility-aware target tracking, achieving scalable and efficient performance with near-baseline accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to improve upon existing methods for offline target tracking trajectory planning by creating scalable, efficient solutions for generating multi-modal tracking datasets and benchmarking tracking planners under consistent, repeatable conditions.

Method: The method combines a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search with optimizations such as obstacle distance caching using Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), beam pruning, and a multi-ray visibility evaluation for efficient trajectory planning on a discretized spatio-temporal grid.

Result: The results show TA star achieves robust visibility performance at significantly reduced computation times, converging on all test scenarios with a 23x average speed-up compared to an unoptimized baseline, and maintaining near-baseline visibility accuracy with negligible performance drop.

Conclusion: TA star is positioned as a practical and scalable offline reference planner for visibility-aware target tracking, demonstrating efficiency and reliability while acknowledging limitations in certain dense environments like Town07.

Abstract: Offline reference trajectories for active target tracking are needed both for building multi-modal tracking datasets and for benchmarking online tracking planners under repeatable conditions. We present Track A star (TA star), an offline search-based trajectory planner that targets the visibility-aware target tracking objective on a discretized four-dimensional spatio-temporal grid (x, y, z, t). TA star combines a layered Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search with three engineering optimizations: cross-time obstacle distance caching against a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. TA star employs a beam-pruned heuristic search on this discrete graph to efficiently find high-quality tracking trajectories. While it trades strict theoretical optimality for practical scalability, our empirical results demonstrate robust, near-baseline visibility performance at a fraction of the computational cost. On a 1000-scenario stress test across eight CARLA Optimized maps, TA star converges on all scenarios and completes in 45 s using 32 workers; on a 248-scenario controlled comparison against an unoptimized priority-queue A star baseline (BinaryHeap implementation) under identical scenario inputs and a 5 x 10^6 expansion cap, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0x and worst-case planning time by 11.8x, while raising convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 baseline-converged subset, TA star changes average visibility by only -0.15 percentage points (pp), with no scenario exceeding a 5 pp drop. We position TA star as a practical offline reference planner under these specific conditions, with limitations and failure cases discussed for environments such as Town07 dense vegetation.

</details>


### [593] [Passive Fault Tolerance through Tension-to-Thrust Feed-Forward: Hybrid Input-to-State Stability for Decentralized Multi-UAV Slung-Load Transport under Abrupt Cable Severance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05339)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Paul Schmitt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a decentralized recovery mechanism for UAV slung-load transport systems to address abrupt cable severances, ensuring stability and recovery without needing peer-state exchange or fixed cable topology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address limitations in existing controllers for multi-UAV slung-load transport, which struggle with fault recovery when cables are severed unexpectedly, requiring decentralized mechanisms for stability and recovery.

Method: The method involves a passive control architecture where cable tension directly informs altitude thrust commands, combined with a proportional-derivative and anti-swing control cascade. A hybrid input-to-state-stability certification is proposed.

Result: Simulations with five UAVs and a payload demonstrate recovery performance with low error metrics (0.312-0.328 m RMSE, 76.1-95.2 mm peak sag) and recovery within one pendulum period after cable severance. The local tension feedback significantly enhances performance compared to disabled controls.

Conclusion: The proposed decentralized control architecture effectively handles abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport, outperforming existing approaches and highlighting the importance of local tension feedback for system stability.

Abstract: Abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport redistributes load and changes the active constraint set, leaving limited time for fault diagnosis and reconfiguration. Existing controllers rely on coordinated force allocation, peer-state exchange, or fixed cable topology, and therefore lack a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced severance. We present a passive architecture that routes each vehicle's measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command, $T_i^{\mathrm{ff}}=T_i$, while a surrounding proportional-derivative, anti-swing, and projection cascade preserves local tracking feasibility. The main contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate that composes a slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction $ρ\in (0,1)$ into an explicit recovery envelope under stated actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions. We validate the controller in Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, a 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and single- and dual-severance schedules: the closed loop attains 0.312-0.328 m RMSE, 76.1-95.2 mm peak sag, and recovery within one payload-pendulum period. Disabling the identity inflates cruise error by 34-39% and peak sag by 3.6x-4.0x, identifying local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the tested decentralized cascade.

</details>


### [594] [Creative Robot Tool Use by Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05411)
*M. Tuluhan Akbulut,Varun Satheesh,Ahmed Jaafar,Alper Ahmetoglu,Shane Parr,Aditya Ganeshan,Shivam Vats,George Konidaris*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper develops a causal reasoning framework that enables robots to use tools in creative ways by identifying causal relationships between the tool and task, grounded in physics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robots to use tools creatively and effectively in situations beyond their primary purpose, thereby enhancing their utility.

Method: A framework is proposed that uses virtual experiments, feature suggestions via visual-language models, counterfactual generation with feature perturbations, causal feature classification, and keypoint-matching for skill transfer.

Result: The framework enables reliable tool selection and effective skill transfer for tasks like reaching, scooping, and stepping by grounding decisions in physics.

Conclusion: Discovering and utilizing causal features tied to physical properties improves both the selection of tools and the transferability of relevant skills.

Abstract: We propose a causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use where a suitable tool for a task is correctly identified for use beyond its primary objectives. The proposed framework first discovers the causal relationships between the tool and the task by conducting simulated experiments in a dynamics model. We decouple the causal discovery problem into two complementary components: VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric and physical feature perturbations. Then, novel objects are classified based on identified causal features, and the tool use skill is transferred via keypoint matching conditioned on the identified causal features. By reconstructing the task in a dynamics model, our approach grounds tool use in the physics of the problem. We illustrate our approach in reaching a distant object with different sticks, scooping candies from a bowl using diverse items, and using different boxes or crates as stepping platforms to retrieve an object from a high shelf. Our baseline comparisons show that identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties leads to more reliable tool selection and stronger skill keypoint transfer.

</details>


### [595] [Contact-Free Grasp Stability Prediction with In-Hand Time-of-Flight Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05461)
*Kyle DuFrene,Cindy Grimm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a contact-free grasp stability predictor using time-of-flight sensors on a robotic gripper, offering faster prediction cycles and high accuracy in classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic grasp planning methods face challenges when exposed to noisy sensors, leading to degradation in performance. The authors aimed to address these limitations by creating a tactile-free solution for predicting grasp stability.

Method: The authors used multi-zone time-of-flight sensors mounted on the distal links of a gripper to classify grasp stability without the need for direct contact. They collected data from real-world grasps across various objects and trained a classifier cycling at a rate of 15 Hz.

Result: Using over 2,500 grasps from 15 objects for training, the approach achieved a classification accuracy of 85.5% on validation and 86.0% on test objects, proving its robustness on unseen objects.

Conclusion: This contact-free method offers a faster, accurate, and sensor noise-resistant solution for grasp stability classification, paving the way for improvements in robotic manipulation efficiency.

Abstract: Current approaches to grasp planning for robotics demonstrate high success rates, but degrade with noisy sensors and other factors. Previous works have proposed tactile-based grasp stability classifiers to detect failures, but these approaches rely on making contact and grasping the object to do so. We propose a contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors mounted in the distal links of a gripper. Our method, as it does not require grasping the object to make a prediction, significantly speeds up the stability classification process, cycling at 15 Hz. We collected over 2,500 real-world grasps across 15 objects to train a classifier. Additionally, we conducted grasp attempts over six additional unseen objects, three for validation and model selection, and three for model testing. Our approach demonstrated strong classification performance, with an accuracy of 85.5% on validation and 86.0% on test objects.

</details>


### [596] [Robust $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Controller Design For INDI-Controlled Quadrotor Using Online Parameter Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05483)
*Tom Aantjes,Till M. Blaha,Spilios Theodoulis,Ewoud J. J. Smeur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper details a robust gain-scheduled controller for quadrotor attitude control using an INDI-based inner loop with online parameter identification, demonstrating stability and effective tracking under uncertainty through simulations and flight tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current gap lies in tuning outer loop gains for an INDI controller in real-time, particularly during uncertain conditions such as flight throws.

Method: The study designs a cascaded attitude controller using signal-based $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ closed-loop shaping, coupled with online system parameter identification for a symmetric quadrotor.

Result: The proposed controller shows good stability margins, effective tracking under uncertainty in nonlinear simulations, and acceptable flight performance during experimental tests, even with challenging parameter values.

Conclusion: A robust gain-scheduled controller effectively enhances the performance of quadrotor attitude control despite parameter uncertainties, proving its viability for real-world applications.

Abstract: It has recently been shown that all physical parameters of an Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller can be estimated onboard a multirotor within half a second, which is fast enough to do the full identification during a throw in the air. However, a robust method to tune outer loop gains for this feedback-linearizing INDI controller depending on the model parameters is still missing. This work presents the design of a robust gain-scheduled controller for attitude control of quadrotor, using an INDI-based inner loop with online identification of its system parameters. A gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with a feedforward filter is synthesized for a symmetric quadrotor using signal-based $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ closed-loop shaping. The resulting controller exhibits good stability margins, with nonlinear simulations confirming effective tracking performance under uncertainty. Experimental evaluation is also conducted through flight tests with full online parameter identification. Even though the identified parameters during these tests are far outside the defined uncertainty range, acceptable flight performance comparable to simulation results is maintained for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

</details>


### [597] [Real-world Latency Analysis of Vehicular Visible Light Communication with Multiple LED Transmitters and an Event-Based Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05541)
*Ryota Soga,Tsukasa Shimizu,Shintaro Shiba,Quan Kong,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces an event-camera-based visible light communication (VLC) system for vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications, addressing challenges like bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter reception, and latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the feasibility of using event cameras in V2X applications due to their high temporal resolution, low latency, and wide dynamic range.

Method: The authors designed a positive-event-only mode, created a protocol to minimize event generation, developed a method for identifying multiple transmitters, and evaluated latency under real scenarios.

Result: The system successfully demonstrated communication distance, multi-transmitter reception from up to three LEDs, and compliance with cooperative perception latency requirements in real vehicular scenarios.

Conclusion: Event-camera-based VLC is both feasible and a viable complement to radio frequency (RF) technologies in V2X systems.

Abstract: Event cameras offer high temporal resolution, low latency, and wide dynamic range, making them promising receivers for visible light communication (VLC) in vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications. This work presents an event-camera-based VLC system addressing three key challenges: bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter reception, and latency characterization.
  We adopt a positive-event-only mode and design a protocol that suppresses event generation while maintaining communication distance and a wide field of view. We also propose a method to identify multiple transmitters and demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs. Finally, we evaluate end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios and show that the system meets cooperative perception requirements. These results demonstrate that event-camera-based VLC is a feasible complement to existing V2X technologies (e.g., RF).

</details>


### [598] [On the Emergence of Pendular Structure in Multi-Contact Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05707)
*Lingxue Lyu,Zihui Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the connection between the Linear Inverted Pendulum Model (LIPM) and legged locomotion control, exploring optimality conditions and testing them on simulations and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make explicit the link between LIPM as a modeling choice and its direct incorporation in control cost functions for legged locomotion.

Method: The study uses a small centroidal Optimal Control Problem (OCP) to analyze angular momentum rate penalties, derives theoretical observations, and validates them on simulations and a quadruped robot.

Result: The analysis reveals behaviors like drift toward pendular force patterns, bounds on angular momentum rates in dynamic stances, and closed-form critical accelerations, and tests them on a Unitree Go1 showing where theory deviates from practice.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that LIPM-like behaviors arise naturally under certain optimality conditions, but practical closed-loop behaviors can deviate, highlighting the need for refined models and understanding.

Abstract: LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a modeling choice rather than as something the
  controller's cost actually prefers. This note tries to make that link more explicit. Working from a small centroidal
  OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like. Three things come
  out. With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at a rate determined by the SVD of the
  moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within 16%. With N=2 stance,
  as in trot, the friction cone introduces a lower bound on $\|\dot{H}_G\|$ that no amount of weight tuning fixes; we
  also see a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration that we can write in closed form. Adding
  a task term that asks for a nonzero $\dot{H}_G$ moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable way. None of
  this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture. We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1
  in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we note where the asymptotic story stops being
  a good description of what the closed loop actually does.

</details>


### [599] [MaMi-HOI: Harmonizing Global Kinematics and Local Geometry for Human-Object Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05756)
*Hao Wang,Shiqi Wang,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces MaMi-HOI, a framework for generating realistic 3D human-object interactions by addressing issues of geometric forgetting and balancing semantic intent with physical constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the issue of generating realistic 3D human-object interactions that harmonize high-level semantic intent and strict low-level physical constraints, where current models fail to maintain precise object contact.

Method: The paper proposes the MaMi-HOI framework with two key components: Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA) to mitigate geometric forgetting by reinjecting dense object details, and Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA) to ensure global kinematic naturalness while preserving precise object contact.

Result: Experiments show that MaMi-HOI achieves natural motion while maintaining precise contact and extends capability to long-term tasks and complex trajectories.

Conclusion: MaMi-HOI effectively bridges the gap between global motion navigation and detailed human-object interaction, advancing realism in 3D scenes for applications like AI and virtual content creation.

Abstract: Generating realistic 3D Human-Object Interactions (HOI) is a fundamental task for applications ranging from embodied AI to virtual content creation, which requires harmonizing high-level semantic intent with strict low-level physical constraints. Existing methods excel at semantic alignment, however, they struggle to maintain precise object contact. We reveal a key finding termed \textit{Geometric Forgetting}: as diffusion model depth increases, semantic feature tend to overshadow object geometry feature, causing the model to lose its perception to object geometry. To address this, we propose MaMi-HOI, a hierarchical framework reconciling \textbf{Ma}cro-level kinematic fluidity with \textbf{Mi}cro-level spatial precision. First, to counteract geometric forgetting, we introduce the Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA), which explicitly re-injects dense object details to perform residual snapping corrections for precise contact. Nevertheless, such aggressive local enforcement can disrupt global dynamics, leading to robotic stiffness. In response, we introduce the Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA), which proactively aligns whole-body posture with spatial objectives, ensuring the skeleton actively accommodates constraints without compromising naturalness. Extensive experiments validate that MaMi-HOI simultaneously achieves natural motion and precise contact. Crucially, it extends generation capabilities to long-term tasks with complex trajectories, effectively bridging the gap between global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes. Code is available at https://github.com/DON738110198/MaMi-HOI.git

</details>


### [600] [Resource-Constrained Robotic Planning in the face of Mixed Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05797)
*Yihao Yin,Pian Yu,Andrea Turrini,Zhiming Chi,Yong Li,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework to create robust strategies for robots operating under uncertainty and resource constraints by combining Consumption Markov Decision Processes with Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces, and provides efficient solution methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face challenges due to various sources of uncertainty and resource constraints in real-world operations, necessitating robust strategy synthesis to ensure smooth task fulfillment without resource exhaustion.

Method: The study models robotic systems using CMDPST to account for uncertainty and resources, integrates the task specifications as LTLf formulas, and offers two strategy synthesis techniques—direct unrolling-based and optimized state-space pruning approaches.

Result: The proposed techniques were validated in experiments within a warehouse transportation network, demonstrating their effectiveness.

Conclusion: The paper successfully synthesizes robust strategies for robots with resource constraints under uncertainties, yielding efficient methods for practical deployment.

Abstract: Robots operate under significant uncertainty, from quantifiable noise to unquantifiable unknowns, and must account for strict operational constraints, such as limited resources. In this paper, we consider the problem of synthesizing robust strategies to guide a robot's actions in fulfilling a given task, while ensuring the system never exhausts its resources. To solve this problem, we first model the robotic system as a Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions(CMDPST), a unified framework modelling nondeterministic actions, quantifiable and unquantifiable uncertainty, and resource consumption. Then, we combine the CMDPST with the task specification, expressed as a Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf ) formula. Lastly, we address the resource constrained optimal robust strategy synthesis problem, which aims to synthesize a strategy that maximizes the probability of satisfying the LTLf objective without resource exhaustion. Our solution involves two techniques: a direct unrolling-based method and a more efficient, optimized approach that leverages state-space pruning for better performance. Experiments on a warehouse transportation network show the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

</details>


### [601] [A Comparative Study of INDI and NDI with Nonlinear Disturbance Observer for Aerial Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05825)
*Benedetta Rota,Mirko Mizzoni,Amr Afifi,Pieter van Goor,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper compares Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) and NDI augmented with a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDI+NDO) for aerial robots using simulations under different operational scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to evaluate and compare the robustness and performance of INDI and NDI+NDO control strategies for fully actuated aerial robots under varying operational challenges.

Method: The study conducts systematic simulations in different scenarios to compare tracking performance, robustness, control effort, and sensitivity to parametric variations, disturbances, and noise.

Result: Results show that INDI is more robust under model mismatches and stress cases, while NDI+NDO is highly effective in nominal conditions but less robust under non-ideal situations.

Conclusion: INDI is recommended for scenarios requiring strong robustness under uncertainties, while NDI+NDO is more suitable for stable and predictable conditions.

Abstract: This work presents a simulation-based comparative robustness analysis of Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) and Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion augmented with a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDI+NDO) for fully actuated aerial robots. A systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios is conducted, where we compare tracking performance, robustness, control effort, under parametric variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise. Results show that INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases, while NDI+NDO primarily matches nominal performance but exhibits greater sensitivity under several non-ideal conditions. These findings provide practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental and observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotic applications.

</details>


### [602] [Cycle-resolved Cephalopod-Inspired Pulsed-Jet Robot With High-Volume Expulsion and Drag-Reduced Gliding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05875)
*Yiyuan Zhang,Anye Zhong,Junkai Chen,Wenci Xin,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a hybrid origami mantle for efficient locomotion. Key focuses include expelled volume, glide duration, and refill dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to mimic cephalopod jet propulsion to better understand and replicate their efficient movement cycle through robotic implementation.

Method: The robot incorporates a rigid-soft origami mantle for contraction, enabling significant cavity volume reduction and drag area minimization. A framework was developed for testing expelled volume, glide, and refill effects.

Result: The robot achieved a peak speed of 0.5 m/s and average speed over 0.2 m/s, validating the importance of contraction, glide, and refill mechanisms in efficient locomotion.

Conclusion: The research showcases a robust robotic system emulating cephalopod propulsion and offers insights into optimizing cycle-resolved pulsed-jet locomotion dynamics.

Abstract: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is not a single isolated expulsion event, but a coordinated cycle involving jet expulsion, passive gliding, and mantle refilling. Inspired by this cycle-resolved biological strategy, this paper presents a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle that enables large, actively driven, and geometry-guided body deformation. The proposed mantle integrates rigid folding panels with a compliant silicone framework, allowing a 75% effective cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and reducing the projected cross-sectional drag area by approximately 75.7% in the contracted gliding configuration. Using this platform, we formulate a cycle-resolved framework to separately investigate how expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway influence whole-cycle locomotion performance. Experiments show that the robot reaches a peak speed of approximately 0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and an average speed exceeding 0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. The results further demonstrate the roles of high expelled-volume-ratio contraction in speed generation, reduced-drag-area gliding under different glide durations, and mantle-aperture-inspired passive inlet valves in assisting refill. This work provides both a robotic implementation of actively deformable cephalopod-like jet propulsion and a unified experimental platform for studying expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics in pulsed-jet locomotion.

</details>


### [603] [Generating Roadside LiDAR Datasets from Vehicle-Side Datasets via Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05897)
*Yuhan Xia,Runxin Zhao,Hanyang Zhuang,Chunxiang Wang,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS), a framework for generating roadside LiDAR datasets from vehicle-side observations to address limited annotated roadside LiDAR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets restricts the development of reliable models for roadside perception in Intelligent Transportation Systems.

Method: The framework uses LiDAR novel view synthesis with vehicle point cloud completion and occupancy-based visibility constraints to generate roadside LiDAR datasets with scalable multi-view rendering.

Result: Experiments demonstrate the synthesized data complements real roadside data, reduces the impact of limited roadside datasets, and enhances the generalization of 3D object detection models to unseen roadside viewpoints.

Conclusion: VRS is an effective approach to enable scalable generation of roadside LiDAR datasets, addressing a key data limitation in enhancing roadside perception models.

Abstract: Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) require reliable environmental perception to support safe and efficient transportation. With the rapid development of Vehicle-to-everything (V2X), roadside perception has become an effective means to extend sensing coverage and improve traffic safety. However, the scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets poses a major challenge for training high-performance roadside perception models. In this paper, we introduce Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS), a data synthesis framework that generates labeled roadside LiDAR datasets from vehicle-side datasets via LiDAR novel view synthesis. To mitigate the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, VRS employs vehicle point cloud completion to compensate for missing geometry in vehicle-side observations, and introduces an occupancy-based visibility constraint to handle large viewpoint changes during cross-view rendering. The proposed framework enables flexible multi-view rendering for scalable roadside data generation. Extensive experiments on roadside 3D object detection demonstrate that the synthesized data effectively complements real roadside data, mitigates the limitations of limited real-world roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

</details>


### [604] [DexSynRefine: Synthesizing and Refining Human-Object Interaction Motion for Physically Feasible Dexterous Robot Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05925)
*Hyesung Lee,Hyunwoo Jung,Si-Hwan Heo,Sungwook Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSynRefine proposes a framework to learn dexterous manipulation from sparse HOI data, addressing the challenges of embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics by coupling synthesis, residual RL policy, and adaptation modules, achieving superior performance in simulations and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of sparse HOI data for dexterous manipulation, such as embodiment mismatch and the lack of dynamic information, and enable scalable learning from human demonstrations.

Method: DexSynRefine combines three components: HOI-MMFP for generating hand-object trajectories, a task-space residual RL policy for grounding synthesized references physically, and a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module for sim-to-real transfer.

Result: The framework consistently outperformed prior baselines and demonstrated successful real-world transfer with a 50-70 percentage points improvement over kinematic retargeting in five dexterous manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: DexSynRefine effectively learns dexterous manipulation from sparse HOI data, addressing critical challenges and enabling scalable robotic applications through robust synthesis and adaptation mechanisms.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation from human-object interaction (HOI) data is a scalable alternative to teleoperation, but HOI demonstrations are sparse and provide only kinematic motion that is not directly executable under embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics. We present DexSynRefine, a framework with three coupled components: HOI-MMFP, a task- and object-initial-state-conditioned extension of motion manifold primitives that synthesizes coordinated hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations; a task-space residual RL policy that physically grounds the synthesized reference while inheriting its kinematic structure; and a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module that enables sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history. Across five dexterous manipulation tasks spanning pick-and-place, tool use, and object reorientation, our task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulations and transfers to a real robot on all five tasks, improving over kinematic retargeting by 50-70 percentage points.

</details>


### [605] [Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion for Universal Goal-Oriented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05960)
*Zhixuan Shen,Yijie Zeng,Shengxiang Luo,Tianrui Li,Haonan Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), a novel model for goal-oriented navigation (GON) in unexplored environments, using map completion with diffusion techniques to enhance both obstacle and semantic label prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches to GON struggle with incomplete maps and inconsistent semantic associations, making localization in unexplored environments challenging.

Method: PLMD employs a diffusion-based model leveraging Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models to complete maps by predicting obstacle and semantic labels based on structural consistency.

Result: PLMD expands unknown map regions, integrates into existing navigation methods, and achieves state-of-the-art results across three GON tasks.

Conclusion: PLMD provides an effective and adaptable solution to the challenges of GON, enhancing map-based navigation strategies through improved map augmentation and goal localization capabilities.

Abstract: In embodied vision, Goal-Oriented Navigation (GON) requires robots to locate a specific goal within an unexplored environment. The primary challenge of GON arises from the need to construct a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) map to understand the environment while simultaneously localizing an unobserved goal. Existing map-based methods typically employ self-centered semantic maps, often facing challenges such as reliance on complete maps or inconsistent semantic association. To this end, we propose Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), which defines a novel map completion diffusion model based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). PLMD generates obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions through a diffusion-based completion process, thereby enabling goal localization even in partially observed environments. Moreover, it mitigates inconsistent semantic association by leveraging structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts and integrating obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process. By substituting predicted labels for unobserved regions, robots can accurately localize the specified objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMD \textbf{(I)} effectively expands the region of unknown maps, \textbf{(II)} integrates seamlessly into existing navigation strategies that rely on semantic maps, \textbf{(III)} achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.

</details>


### [606] [Accurate Trajectory Tracking with MPCC for Flapping-Wing MAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06042)
*Charbel Toumieh,Jack Zeng,Niel Mistry,Dario Floreano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper develops a real-time Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) for flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles to achieve precise autonomous control with compact models addressing coupled aerodynamics, delivering notable improvements in trajectory tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The difficulty of achieving precise autonomous control for bird-scale ornithopters due to coupled dynamics in lift, airspeed, and turning authority.

Method: Introduced a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) approach using a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures key aerodynamic couplings, enabling real-time predictive optimization without predefined timing.

Result: Validation tests on the XFly ornithopter demonstrated a mean deviation of 6.5 to 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s along complex trajectories, significantly outperforming previous methods.

Conclusion: The proposed MPCC framework achieved a high level of accuracy in ornithopter control, resolving persistent tracking errors and eliminating the need for predefined speed profiles.

Abstract: Flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles offer quieter and safer operation than rotary-wing drones, yet achieving precise autonomous control of bird-scale ornithopters remains challenging: lift, airspeed, and turning authority are tightly coupled and governed by only a few control inputs. Conventional cascaded controllers treat altitude, speed, and heading independently, producing persistent tracking errors during complex maneuvers, while time-parameterized trajectory tracking requires predefined speed profiles that existing methods cannot robustly produce for these coupled dynamics. We address both limitations simultaneously with a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) approach that tracks arc-length-parameterized trajectories while optimizing progress online, eliminating the need for predefined timing. However, MPCC requires a dynamical model that captures the coupled aerodynamics without exceeding the computational budget of real-time nonlinear optimization. Here, we propose a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures the dominant couplings of bird-scale ornithopters, enabling real-time predictive control. We validated the method with the XFly ornithopter flying along circular and three-dimensional racing trajectories and achieved a mean deviation from the reference trajectory between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, which represents an almost 10-fold improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

</details>


### [607] [Monitoring autonomous persistent surveillance missions using invariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06062)
*Vladislav Nenchev,Prodromos Sotiriadis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper develops a runtime monitoring framework for autonomous robots performing persistent surveillance by treating their autonomy as a black box.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for effective runtime monitoring in robotic surveillance tasks, particularly when the robot's autonomy stack is opaque (black box), ensuring effective system evaluation.

Method: A state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter varying dynamics is modeled for the environment and a monitor is designed via offline computation of invariant sets. To scale for larger environments, a compositional decentralized approach is used for computing low-dimensional invariant sets and checking their conjunctions online.

Result: The monitoring approach is validated through a case study involving a real robot monitoring a labyrinth, demonstrating soundness, completeness, and practical applicability.

Conclusion: The proposed compositional runtime monitoring framework effectively ensures persistent surveillance by autonomous robots in complex environments.

Abstract: This paper studies runtime monitoring for persistent surveillance by autonomous robots when the autonomy stack is a black box. The environment is partitioned into finitely many parts, each carrying an uncertainty state that decreases when observed and increases otherwise. We model the closed loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter varying dynamics and design a monitor based on an invariant computed offline. As this invariant is typically hard to obtain for large to-be-surveyed spaces, we propose a compositional monitor obtained by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariant sets for each uncertainty region, and checking their conjunction online. Under common independence assumptions, the compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant. The approach is applied in a case study with a real robot persistently monitoring a labyrinth, emphasizing its applicability in practice.

</details>


### [608] [VLA-GSE: Boosting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning in VLA with Generalized and Specialized Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06175)
*Yuhua Jiang,Junjie Lu,Xinyao Qin,Xiaoyu Chen,Kaixin Wang,Feifei Gao,Li Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces VLA-GSE, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for vision-language-action models, enabling better robotic control adaptation and knowledge preservation using expert-based decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language-action models face challenges in adapting pre-trained knowledge for robotic control due to risks of overfitting and forgetting. Parameter-efficient methods partially address this, but further improvement is needed in effective adaptation.

Method: The study proposes VLA-GSE, a novel framework that decomposes frozen vision-language backbones into generalized and specialized experts based on singular components. This approach balances adaptation capabilities and maintains knowledge preservation under a restricted parameter budget.

Result: VLA-GSE updates only 2.51% of model parameters while achieving superior performance compared to strong baselines. It demonstrated 81.2% zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserved vision-language model capabilities on multimodal benchmarks, and improved manipulation success under distribution shifts.

Conclusion: VLA-GSE framework effectively combines control adaptation and pre-trained knowledge preservation, advancing robotic tasks and multimodal understanding. It offers a lightweight solution outperforming traditional and existing parameter-efficient tuning methods.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models inherit rich visual-semantic priors from pre-trained vision-language backbones, but adapting them to robotic control remains challenging. Full fine-tuning (FFT) is prone to overfitting on downstream robotic data and catastrophic forgetting of pretrained vision-language capabilities. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) better preserves pre-trained knowledge, yet existing PEFT methods still struggle to adapt effectively to robot control tasks. To address this gap, we propose VLA-GSE, a parameter-efficient VLA fine-tuning framework that improves control adaptation while retaining PEFT's knowledge preservation advantage. Specifically, VLA-GSE (Generalized and Specialized Experts) is initialized by spectrally decomposing the frozen backbone, assigning leading singular components to generalized experts (shared experts) and disjoint residual components to specialized experts (routed experts). This decomposition improves adaptation capacity under a fixed trainable-parameter budget. Under a comparable parameter budget, VLA-GSE updates only 2.51% of the full model parameters and consistently outperforms strong FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pre-trained VLM capability comparably to LoRA on multimodal understanding benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation success under multiple distribution shifts. Code is available at: https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/VLA-GSE

</details>


### [609] [When to Trust Imagination: Adaptive Action Execution for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06222)
*Rui Wang,Yue Zhang,Jiehong Lin,Kuncheng Luo,Jianan Wang,Zhongrui Wang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a verifier for adaptive execution in robotic manipulation by assessing the consistency between predicted and observed realities, leading to efficient and robust outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current World Action Models (WAMs) employ fixed action sequences after prediction, leaving robots unable to adjust when actual outcomes deviate from predicted futures. This can reduce robustness and responsiveness during operation.

Method: The authors formulated adaptive WAM execution as a future-reality verification problem. They propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC) to assess the reliability of predicted actions and adapt chunk sizes dynamically. They also introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training for better trajectory coverage.

Result: FFDC achieved significant performance improvements: on the RoboTwin benchmark, WAM forward passes decreased by 69.10%, execution time reduced by 34.02%, and success rate marginally increased by 2.54%; while in real-world tests, success rates improved by 35%.

Conclusion: FFDC offers a strong robustness-efficiency tradeoff for robotic manipulation by enabling the adaptive execution of WAMs based on prediction-observation consistency, suggesting its promise for improving real-world robotic applications.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by jointly predicting future visual observations and future actions. However, current WAMs typically execute a fixed number of predicted actions after each model inference, leaving the robot blind to whether the imagined future remains consistent with the actual physical rollout. In this work, we formulate adaptive WAM execution as a future-reality verification problem: the robot should execute longer when the WAM-predicted future remains reliable, and replan earlier when reality deviates from imagination. To this end, we propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate whether the remaining action rollout can still be trusted. FFDC enables adaptive action chunk sizes as an emergent consequence of prediction-observation consistency, preserving the efficiency of long-horizon execution while restoring responsiveness in contact-rich or difficult phases. We further introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training to improve long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution. Experiments on the RoboTwin benchmark and in the real world demonstrate that our method achieves a strong robustness-efficiency trade-off: on RoboTwin, it reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10% and execution time by 34.02%, while improving success rate by 2.54% over the short-chunk baseline; in real-world experiments, it improves success rate by 35%.

</details>


### [610] [RobotEQ: Transitioning from Passive Intelligence to Active Intelligence in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06234)
*Kuofei Fang,Xinyi Che,Haomin Ouyang,Shufan Zhang,Xuehao Wang,Qi Liu,Liyi Liu,Chenqi Zhang,Wenxi Cai,Wenyu Dai,Jinyang Wu,Fan Zhang,Haoyu Chen,Bin He,Zheng Lian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces RobotEQ, a benchmark designed to evaluate active intelligence in robots by assessing their understanding of social norms across various scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this work is to address the gap in robotic systems by enabling them to understand and adhere to social norms autonomously, without explicit user instructions.

Method: The method involves creating RobotEQ-Data, a dataset of 1,900 egocentric images with detailed annotations, and a benchmarking framework (RobotEQ-Bench) to evaluate current AI models in tasks like action judgment and spatial grounding.

Result: Experimental findings reveal that state-of-the-art models currently struggle with active intelligence, particularly in spatial grounding, but incorporating external social norm knowledge bases using RAG techniques improves performance.
