Abstract: Developers often make similar mistakes across code locations implementing related functionalities. These locations, called siblings, share similar issues and require similar fixes. Accurately identifying siblings and consistently repairing them are crucial for automated program repair. Hercules is a SOTA technique designed for sibling repair. However, it is limited by strong assumptions about sibling locations and commit-history availability, rigid AST-based sibling matching, and inflexible template-based patch generation. To address these limitations, we present SiblingRepair, a new LLM-based multi-hunk APR technique specialized for sibling repair. Starting from a suspicious location identified by spectrum-based fault localization, SiblingRepair searches for semantically related sibling candidates using token- and embedding-based code matching, without restricting discovery to failing-test coverage or commit history. It then uses an LLM to identify failure-relevant siblings and generate consistent patches through two complementary strategies: simultaneous repair, which jointly repairs siblings, and iterative repair, which progressively analyzes candidates for patch construction. SiblingRepair further preserves promising patches generated from earlier suspicious locations and combines them into generalized multi-hunk patches. We evaluate SiblingRepair on the Defects4J and GHRB benchmarks. The results show that SiblingRepair substantially outperforms SOTA multi-hunk repair techniques including Hercules. Our evaluation further demonstrates its repair efficiency, the effectiveness of its sibling detection and repair components, and limited impact of the LLM data leakage on the results. Overall, SiblingRepair advances automated sibling and general multi-hunk repair.

</details>


### [639] [Correct Code, Vulnerable Dependencies: A Large Scale Measurement Study of LLM-Specified Library Versions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06279)
*Chengjie Wang,Jingzheng Wu,Xiang Ling,Tianyue Luo,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper studies the risks associated with version selection in LLM-generated Python code, focusing on vulnerabilities and compatibility issues with third-party libraries. It evaluates 10 LLMs on their version choices and impacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With LLMs being increasingly used in software development, the authors aim to assess the security and compatibility risks involved in the versioning of third-party libraries in the generated code, a topic not systematically addressed before.

Method: The study employs PinTrace, a benchmark of 1,000 Stack Overflow programming tasks, to measure version-level risks. It evaluates 10 LLMs' proclivities for specifying library versions and analyzes security vulnerabilities, compatibility failures, and the effects of externally constrained version requirements.

Result: LLMs specify TPL versions inconsistently but converge on a small set of risky versions, leading to 36.70%-55.70% of known CVEs and poor compatibility. Externally anchored version constraints reduced risks significantly.

Conclusion: The findings highlight version selection in LLM-generated code as a significant, previously overlooked risk. Improved practices in anchoring versions can mitigate vulnerabilities and compatibility problems in LLM-driven coding tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are now largely involved in software development workflows, and the code they generate routinely includes third-party library (TPL) imports annotated with specific version identifiers. These version choices can carry security and compatibility risks, yet they have not been systematically studied.
  We present the first large-scale measurement study of version-level risk in LLM-generated Python code, evaluating 10 LLMs on PinTrace, a curated benchmark of 1,000 Stack Overflow programming tasks. LLMs tend to specify version identifiers when directly prompted at 26.83%-95.18%, while down to 6.45%-59.19% in creating a manifest file directly. Among the specified versions, 36.70%-55.70% of tasks contain at least one known CVE, and 62.75%-74.51% of them carry Critical or High severity ratings. In 72.27%-91.37% of cases, the associated CVEs were publicly disclosed before the model's knowledge cutoff. The statistics show all models converge on the same small set of risky release versions, indicating a systemic bias rather than isolated model error. Static compatibility rates range from 19.70% to 63.20%, with installation failure as the dominant cause. The dynamic test cases confirm the pattern by 6.49%-48.62% pass rates. Further experiments confirm that these failures are attributable to version selection rather than code quality, and that externally anchored version constraints substantially reduce both vulnerability exposure and compatibility failures. Our findings reveal LLM version selection as a first-class, previously overlooked risk surface in LLM-based development. We disclosed these findings to the community of the evaluated models, and several confirmed the issue. All the code and dataset have been released for open science at https://github.com/dw763j/PinTrace.

</details>


### [640] [Constraint Decay: The Fragility of LLM Agents in Backend Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06445)
*Francesco Dente,Dario Satriani,Paolo Papotti*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper explores the limitations of large language model (LLM) agents in generating structurally constrained multi-file backend code, discovering significant performance degradation under complex requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the performance of LLM agents in generating software that adheres to strict structural constraints, as real-world software must meet non-functional requirements beyond functional correctness.

Method: The study evaluated agents on 100 backend generation tasks across eight web frameworks. Tasks were analyzed using behavioral tests and static verifiers under a fixed API contract to isolate structural complexity effects.

Result: Agents faced performance degradation when structural complexity increased. They excelled in minimal frameworks like Flask but struggled with convention-heavy ones like Django. A phenomenon termed 'constraint decay' was revealed.

Conclusion: LLM agents exhibit significant challenges in meeting both functional and structural requirements, with structural constraints posing a key barrier to their effectiveness in production-grade code generation.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents demonstrate strong performance in autonomous code generation under loose specifications. However, production-grade software requires strict adherence to structural constraints, such as architectural patterns, databases, and object-relational mappings. Existing benchmarks often overlook these non-functional requirements, rewarding functionally correct but structurally arbitrary solutions. We present a systematic study evaluating how well agents handle structural constraints in multi-file backend generation. By fixing a unified API contract across 80 greenfield generation tasks and 20 feature-implementation tasks spanning eight web frameworks, we isolate the effect of structural complexity using a dual evaluation with end-to-end behavioral tests and static verifiers. Our findings reveal a phenomenon of constraint decay: as structural requirements accumulate, agent performance exhibits a substantial decline. Capable configurations lose 30 points on average in assertion pass rates from baseline to fully specified tasks, while some weaker configurations approach zero. Framework sensitivity analysis exposes significant performance disparities: agents succeed in minimal, explicit frameworks (e.g., Flask) but perform substantially worse on average in convention-heavy environments (e.g., FastAPI, Django). Finally, error analysis identifies data-layer defects (e.g., incorrect query composition and ORM runtime violations) as the leading root causes. This work highlights that jointly satisfying functional and structural requirements remains a key open challenge for coding agents.

</details>


### [641] [To What Extent Does Agent-generated Code Require Maintenance? An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06464)
*Shota Sawada,Tatsuya Shirai,Yutaro Kashiwa,Ken'ichi Yamaguchi,Hiroshi Iwata,Hajimu Iida*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper examines the maintainability of AI-generated code, finding it requires less maintenance than human-authored code, with updates focusing on feature extensions and primarily maintained by human developers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address unanswered questions about the long-term maintainability of AI-generated code in software development.

Method: Analyzed over 1,000 AI-generated files and approximately 3,200 changes from the AIDev dataset and 100 popular GitHub repositories.

Result: AI-generated files are maintained less frequently, focus on feature extensions, and rely heavily on human developers for updates.

Conclusion: AI-generated code is less frequently maintained than human-authored code, with human developers playing a critical role in its ongoing updates and maintenance.

Abstract: LLM-based autonomous coding agents have reshaped software development. While these agents excel at code generation, open questions persist about the long-term maintainability of AI-generated code. This study empirically investigates the maintenance extent, human involvement, and modification types of AI-generated files versus human-authored code. Using the AIDev dataset of AI-generated pull requests and GitHub, we analyzed over 1,000 files and approximately 3,200 changes from 100 popular repositories. Our findings show that: (i) AI-generated files receive less frequent maintenance than human-authored code, with updates affecting only a small fraction of file size; (ii) the most frequent modifications to AI code are feature extensions, whereas human updates focus on bug fixes, and (iii) human developers perform the large majority of this maintenance.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [642] [Decoding Alignment without Encoding Alignment: A critique of similarity analysis in neuroscience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05907)
*Johannes Bertram,Luciano Dyballa,T. Anderson Keller,Savik Kinger,Steven W. Zucker*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper critiques the commonly used decoding approaches in neuroscience and machine learning, pointing out their potential to misrepresent neuron population structures due to reliance on small subpopulations. It proposes encoding paradigms as complementary metrics to capture the broader neuronal organization and function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the misleading assumption that decoding-based representational geometry accurately reflects the neuronal population's organization and computation.

Method: The authors introduce experiments in biological systems and deep learning models, including controlled MNIST tests, to demonstrate the shortcomings of decoding approaches and validate the significance of encoding manifold topology.

Result: The study reveals that decoding metrics can remain unchanged despite causal manipulations of encoding topology, highlighting their insensitivity to distributional aspects of function across neurons; decoding methods can misinterpret population-level computation.

Conclusion: Decoding behavior similarity does not equate to computational or functional similarity; encoding manifolds should be adopted to complement decoding metrics for robust comparisons of neural systems.

Abstract: Decoding approaches are widely used in neuroscience and machine learning to compare stimulus representations across neural systems, such as different brain regions, organisms, and deep learning models. Popular methods include decoding (perceptual) manifolds and alignment metrics such as Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and Dynamic Similarity Analysis (DSA), where similarity in decoding representations is interpreted as evidence for similar computation. This paper demonstrates a fundamental weakness behind this approach: it is misleading to assume that representational geometry is representative of a neuronal population as a whole, when such representations may actually be shaped by a very small subset of neurons. We show that the complementary encoding paradigm addresses this issue directly: it characterizes how neurons are organized globally in terms of their responses to a set of data, providing insight into how the decoding representation is implemented by neurons within a population. We demonstrate across experiments in biological systems and deep learning models that (i) surprisingly, similar decoding behavior and high representational alignment can arise from small, non-representative subpopulations of neurons; and critically, (ii) alignment metrics are insensitive to encoding manifold topology (how function is distributed across neurons), despite this being a key signature of differentiation across biological systems. A controlled MNIST experiment provides causal evidence: decoding metrics remain unchanged even when encoding topology is causally manipulated via the training loss. Overall, similarity in decoding behavior, as measured by classic alignment metrics, does not imply similarity in function or computation, motivating the use of encoding manifolds as a complementary tool for comparing neural systems.

</details>


### [643] [A multi-scale information geometry reveals the structure of mutual information in neural populations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06304)
*Simone Azeglio,Steeve Laquitaine,Ulisse Ferrari,Matthew Chalk*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unique Riemannian representational geometry for neural population codes, rooted in information theory and capturing encoding structure across multiple scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to develop a principled framework for understanding how neural population responses encode sensory stimuli, overcoming limitations of existing methods which produce varying conclusions.

Method: The authors define a Riemannian geometry based on how distances in stimulus space contract through coarse-graining, leading to a multi-scale Fisher information metric. They estimate this metric using diffusion models and apply it to real-world neural data.

Result: The proposed framework reveals encoding structures across scales, directly relates to mutual information, and identifies interpretable stimulus features. Applied to visual data, it highlights features crucial for information transmission.

Conclusion: This work offers a robust, scalable method to analyze neural population codes, providing insights into encoding mechanisms and uncovering meaningful stimulus dimensions.

Abstract: Understanding how neural population responses represent sensory information is a central problem in systems neuroscience. One approach is to define a representational geometry on stimulus space in which distances reflect how reliably stimuli can be distinguished from neural activity. However, different constructions of these distances can lead to qualitatively different conclusions about the neural code. Here, we show that a unique Riemannian representational geometry emerges from first principles governing how distances contract as stimulus resolution is lost through coarse-graining. This results in a multi-scale extension of the Fisher information metric, capturing encoding structure from fine stimulus details to coarse global distinctions. The resulting geometry is exactly related to the mutual information encoded by the population: well encoded stimulus directions - those contributing more to mutual information - are expanded, whereas poorly encoded directions are contracted. The metric tensor can be estimated using diffusion models, making the framework practical for large neural populations and high-dimensional stimuli. Applied to visual cortical responses to natural images, the eigenvectors of the metric tensor identify stimulus variations that contribute most to information transmission, yielding interpretable features that are robust to modelling choices. Together, these results provide a principled, information-theoretic framework for characterising neural population codes.

</details>


### [644] [Beyond Object-Level Alignment: Do Brains and DNNs Preserve the Same Transformations?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06420)
*Yukiyasu Kamitani*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new way to compare brains and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) called "approximate naturality," focusing on whether both preserve transformations among stimuli, measured using a new metric called Naturality Violation Score (NVS).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods of brain-DNN alignment focus on stimulus-level correspondence or geometric properties, but the authors want to explore whether both systems preserve transformation structures.

Method: They propose a framework using approximate naturality, where transformations are followed across brain and DNN pathways and compared for alignment using the Naturality Violation Score (NVS).

Result: The new NVS metric can distinguish varied alignment issues, successfully applied to analyzing five human subjects' fMRI data with three vision DNNs, showing different alignments for semantic versus visual stimulus axes.

Conclusion: NVS provides a novel way to assess brain-DNN alignment by focusing on preserved transformations rather than static similarities, enabling deeper comparisons and opening opportunities for broader analyses.

Abstract: Brain-DNN alignment is usually assessed through stimulus-level correspondence or stimulus-set geometry. Inspired by category theory, we operationalize a different question: do brain and model preserve the same candidate transformations among stimuli? We formalize this as approximate naturality: if a proxy-defined stimulus change is propagated through the brain side and then translated to the model side, the result should match translating first and then propagating, so that the naturality square approximately commutes. We quantify deviations from commutativity by a Naturality Violation Score (NVS) normalized to a permutation null, shifting alignment from per-stimulus sameness to preservation of structure under an explicitly chosen comparison map. As a proof of concept, a controlled five-factor synthetic setting shows that NVS separates complementary alignment failures that aggregate object- and geometry-level scalars cannot resolve. Applied to fMRI responses from the GOD dataset (5 subjects), 3 vision DNNs, and 3 World-Model proxy embeddings, the axis-resolved analysis reveals a hierarchy crossover: semantic axes align most strongly toward HVC and deeper DNN layers (NVS^animacy = 0.39 vs 0.52 for the next-best axis and 1.0 for the permutation-null baseline), whereas low- and mid-level visual axes align toward earlier visual cortex and shallower layers. Supporting analyses (a 15-axis appendix atlas, dissociation tests against RSA/CKA and encoding/decoding accuracy, and a W-less anchor-ablation control) confirm that the alignment is selective over candidate morphism families rather than uniform. NVS thereby turns brain-DNN comparison into a test of jointly preserved candidate transformations, relative to an explicit proxy space and permutation null, and opens a path to richer proxy spaces and controlled world-side transformations.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [645] [Maximizing Rollout Informativeness under a Fixed Budget: A Submodular View of Tree Search for Tool-Use Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05262)
*Yuelin Hu,Zhenbo Yu,Zhengxue Cheng,Wei Liu,Li Song*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel framework (InfoTree) for optimizing tool-use rollouts in policy optimization by leveraging Uncertainty-aware Upper Confidence Bound (UUCB) and Adaptive Budget Allocator (ABA), and demonstrates superior performance across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional samplers face limitations in maintaining informativeness under fixed computational budgets, especially for challenging prompts. The study aims to overcome these limitations with a more efficient approach to intermediate state selection and rollout strategy.

Method: The authors formalize Rollout Informativeness under a Fixed Budget (RIFB), use monotone submodular maximization for state selection, and propose training-time enhancements like UUCB and ABA to optimize rollouts. Additionally, they incorporate Speculative Expansion to reduce computational overhead.

Result: InfoTree framework consistently outperforms existing methods like flat GRPO, DeepSearch, and Tree-GRPO across various benchmarks in reasoning, web-search agents, and coding tools. It improves mixed-outcome ratios, reduces computational overhead, and proves robust across hyperparameter settings.

Conclusion: The proposed InfoTree framework successfully demonstrates enhanced informativeness, efficiency, and robustness in handling diverse tasks under fixed computational budgets, making it a promising tool for policy optimization research.

Abstract: We formalize Rollout Informativeness under a Fixed Budget (RIFB) as the expected non-vanishing policy-gradient mass that a tool-use rollout set injects into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We prove that any budget-agnostic independent sampler suffers a collapse rate bounded away from zero for hard prompts regardless of the budget. Motivated by this, we recast intermediate state selection as a monotone submodular maximization problem, where a greedy one-step selector enjoys a 1 minus 1/e approximation guarantee.
  Our Uncertainty-aware Upper Confidence Bound (UUCB) terms arise as closed-form marginal gains of this objective. This turns the token-level entropy bonus from an empirical trick into an analytic consequence of the formulation. We present InfoTree, a training-time tree-search framework coupling UUCB with a learned Adaptive Budget Allocator (ABA) and an asynchronous Speculative Expansion scheme.
  ABA rescues prompts whose initial tree is wasted on uniform outcomes, lifting the mixed-outcome ratio from 58.1 percent to 76.3 percent with less than 5 percent budget overhead. Speculative Expansion reduces wall-clock overhead from 14.3 percent to 4.8 percent by tolerating bounded staleness in UUCB scores.
  Across nine benchmarks spanning math reasoning (AIME 2024 and 2025, MATH-500, OlympiadBench, USAMO), web-search agents (GAIA, HLE-100, BrowseComp-lite), and tool-rich coding and OS agents (APPS-verified, AgentBench-OS), InfoTree outperforms flat GRPO, DeepSearch, Tree-GRPO, AT2PO, CW-GRPO, and RC-GRPO. Head-to-head compositions with Tree-GRPO prefix sharing and CW-GRPO contribution weights deliver further gains, confirming that our selector operates orthogonally to rollout reuse and trajectory re-weighting. A 5 by 5 by 5 robustness grid reveals that over three quarters of the hyperparameter space lies on a performance plateau, confirming UUCB robustness.

</details>


### [646] [Forecasting Oncology Demand Trends with Boosting-Based Bayesian Conjugate Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05270)
*Ademir Batista dos Santos Neto,Tiago Alessandro Espinola Ferreira,Paulo Renato Alves Firmino*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Bayesian framework with boosting mechanisms for forecasting oncology demand trends, outperforming traditional approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for accurate healthcare trend forecasting to improve planning and resource allocation.

Method: Utilizing a Bayesian framework with Poisson-Gamma modeling, coupled with a residual-based boosting mechanism for trend capturing.

Result: The proposed model significantly surpasses established forecasting methods in accuracy, achieving a 38.25% gain in correct trend direction detection.

Conclusion: Bayesian modeling with residual boosting proves effective for dynamic healthcare forecasting, suggesting its potential for broader applications.

Abstract: Accurate trend forecasting in healthcare time series is essential for planning and resource allocation. This paper proposes a Bayesian framework for predicting oncology demand trends, modeling weekly appointments as a Poisson process with a Gamma prior to the demand rate. To enhance adaptability and capture persistent directional patterns, we incorporate a residual-based boosting mechanism grounded in a Gamma-Log-Normal conjugate structure. This boosting approach allows the model to track both short- and long-term trend shifts while maintaining the analytical tractability of conjugate Bayesian updating. The methodology was evaluated on real oncology service data from Cariri, Ceara, Brazil, and compared against established baselines, including linear regression, ARIMA, naive forecasting, LSTM neural networks, and XGBoost. Results showed that the proposed model outperforms competing methods in trend detection accuracy, with gains in terms of percentage of correct direction of 38.25% in relation to the second best approach in some cases.

</details>


### [647] [Estimating Implicit Regularization in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05436)
*Joseph H. Rudoler,Kevin Tan,Giles Hooker,Konrad P. Kording*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper discusses how implicit regularization, which affects deep learning systems, can be empirically estimated using gradient matching methods, aiding both theoretical understanding and practical network analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding implicit regularization is crucial for optimizing deep learning systems because it explains why models favor simpler solutions and helps theorists and practitioners optimize training processes.

Method: The paper introduces gradient matching methods to empirically estimate the implicit regularization effects, verifying them on networks with known explicit and implicit penalties.

Result: Gradient matching successfully recovered explicit penalties like $ll_1$ and $ll_2$, replicated effects like quadratic weight penalty from early stopping, and characterized the implicit effects of dropout in deep networks.

Conclusion: This empirical method enables practitioners and theorists to analyze complex implicit regularization in modern deep learning systems, making it a versatile tool for network optimization.

Abstract: Deep learning systems are known to exhibit implicit regularization (alt. implicit bias), favoring simple solutions instead of merely minimizing the loss function. In some cases, we can analytically derive the implicit regularization -- connecting it to an equivalent penalty that augments the learning objective. However, modern deep learning systems are complex, carrying modifications to the training procedure and architecture (e.g. early stopping, minibatching, dropout) whose effects are not always directly interpretable. Although estimating the resulting implicit regularization could aid theorists in algorithm design and practitioners in interpreting their hyperparameter choices, this problem has received little direct attention. It is also tractable: regularization makes weight updates deviate from loss gradients, promising a signal for identifying implicit bias. Here we provide gradient matching methods that can be used to empirically estimate the implicit regularization. Our method works on networks with known regularization, recovering popular explicit penalties like $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$. It also replicates known implicit effects, like the quadratic weight penalty induced by early stopping in gradient descent, demonstrating that it can be used to test theories of implicit regularization. Crucially, because our method is empirical, it can handle implicit regularization in arbitrary networks. We demonstrate this use by characterizing the effects of dropout in deep networks, showing implicit $\ell_2$ effects in this popular method. Our work shows that practitioners can use gradient matching to understand regularization in networks with implicit biases that are too complicated to derive analytically.

</details>


### [648] [Convexity in Disguise: A Theoretical Framework for Nonconvex Low-Rank Matrix Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05446)
*Chengyu Cui,Gongjun Xu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a theoretical framework that explains why nonconvex methods are effective for low-rank matrix estimation, focusing on a disguised convexity using a benign regularizer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-rank matrix estimation is a key challenge in machine learning and AI for learning high-dimensional data, but existing analyses for nonconvex methods often require additional regularization and problem-specific arguments.

Method: The authors propose a framework that uses a benign regularizer to ensure an equivalent locally strongly convex formulation without modifying the original update rule, revealing a disguised convexity in nonconvex methods.

Result: The framework explains the effectiveness of nonconvex procedures across a broad range of low-rank matrix estimation problems, eliminating the need for practical regularization.

Conclusion: Nonconvex methods for low-rank matrix estimation can be theoretically guaranteed due to an inherent disguised convexity, offering broader applicability without additional complexities.

Abstract: Nonconvex methods have emerged as a dominant approach for low-rank matrix estimation, a problem that arises widely in machine learning and AI for learning and representing high-dimensional data. Existing analyses for these methods often require additional regularization to mitigate nonconvexity, even though such regularization is often unnecessary in practice. Moreover, most analyses rely on problem-specific arguments that are difficult to generalize to more complex settings. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for studying nonconvex procedures across a broad class of low-rank matrix estimation problems. Rather than focusing on a specific model, we reveal a fundamental mechanism that explains why nonconvex procedures can behave well in low-rank estimation. Our key device is a {\it benign regularizer} that does not alter the original update rule, but yields an equivalent locally strongly convex formulation of the algorithm. This perspective uncovers a disguised convexity inherent in the nonconvex procedure and provides a new route to theoretical guarantees for nonconvex low-rank matrix estimation.

</details>


### [649] [Permutation-preserving Functions and Neural Vecchia Covariance Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05523)
*Jian Cao,Nian Liu,Ying Lin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: A framework is proposed for learning scalable covariance kernels for Gaussian processes by utilizing deep neural networks with Vecchia approximations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable expressive and non-stationary Gaussian process kernel learning that is scalable and computationally efficient.

Method: Design neural architectures that respect permutation symmetry and leverage Vecchia factorization to model kriging coefficients and conditional standard deviations.

Result: The proposed approach improves training stability and data efficiency while maintaining scalability in Gaussian process modeling.

Conclusion: The paper bridges classical Gaussian process methodologies with modern deep learning techniques, achieving enhanced kernel learning capabilities.

Abstract: We introduce a novel framework for constructing scalable and flexible covariance kernels for Gaussian processes (GPs) by directly learning the covariance structure under a regression-type parameterization induced by Vecchia approximations, using deep neural architectures. Specifically, we model kriging coefficients and conditional standard deviations, deterministic quantities that uniquely characterize the covariance, providing stable and informative learning targets. Exploiting the permutation-equivariant structure of conditioning sets in the Vecchia factorization, we derive a universal representation for permutation-preserving functions and design neural architectures that respect this symmetry, leading to improved training stability and data efficiency. The proposed approach enables expressive, non-stationary kernel learning while maintaining computational scalability, thereby bridging classical GP methodology with modern deep learning.

</details>


### [650] [Relaxed Sparsest-Permutation Formulation for Causal Discovery at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05568)
*Sunmin Oh,Sang-Yun Oh,Gunwoong Park*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces SCOPE, a scalable pipeline for learning causal structures in large linear structural equation models by relaxing exact Cholesky factorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal structure learning is computationally demanding when applied to large datasets, necessitating scalable solutions.

Method: The method modifies sparsest-permutation learning by introducing support-level relaxation and a precision-support screening graph, utilizing incomplete Cholesky factorization efficiently.

Result: SCOPE achieves comparable Markov equivalence class recovery accuracy to slower methods while reducing runtime and scaling to datasets with up to 10,000 variables.

Conclusion: The relaxed formulation enables scalable and robust causal structure learning, making SCOPE effective for large-scale applications.

Abstract: Despite the growing availability of large datasets, causal structure learning remains computationally prohibitive at scale. We revisit sparsest-permutation learning for linear structural equation models and show that exact Cholesky factorization is unnecessary for structure recovery. This observation motivates a support-level relaxation that searches for sparse triangular factors over a precision-support screening graph. The relaxed formulation can be efficiently evaluated via masked zero-fill incomplete Cholesky factorization, enabling scalable comparison of candidate orderings. At the population level, we establish soundness for Markov equivalence class (MEC) recovery under no-cancellation and sparsest Markov representation assumptions, as well as robustness to ordering misspecification. Motivated by these guarantees, we introduce SCOPE, a sparse-Cholesky pipeline that provides a scalable implementation of the relaxed formulation. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate that SCOPE matches the MEC recovery accuracy of substantially slower baselines, while achieving significantly reduced runtime and scaling to 10k variables.

</details>


### [651] [In-Context Positive-Unlabeled Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05591)
*Siyan Liu,Yi Chang,Manli Cheng,Qinglong Tian,Pengfei Li*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents PUICL, a pretrained transformer for Positive-Unlabeled (PU) learning that performs classification tasks via in-context learning without dataset-specific training or optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PU methods require dataset-specific training or optimization, making them inefficient for quick, versatile task-solving. The paper aims to simplify and improve the applicability of PU learning.

Method: The authors develop PUICL, pretrained on synthetic PU datasets generated through structural causal models, enabling it to learn diverse feature-label relationships and class distributions. It performs inference via in-context learning, processing inputs in one forward pass without gradient updates.

Result: PUICL surpasses existing PU learning baselines in terms of AUC and accuracy on 20 semi-synthetic benchmarks, demonstrating competitive F1-scores.

Conclusion: PUICL showcases how in-context learning can extend beyond supervised learning to PU classification, offering a more efficient and robust alternative to traditional methods.

Abstract: Positive-unlabeled (PU) learning addresses binary classification when only a set of labeled positives is available alongside a pool of unlabeled samples drawn from a mixture of positives and negatives. Existing PU methods typically require dataset-specific training or iterative optimization, which limits their applicability when many tasks must be solved quickly or with little tuning. We introduce PUICL, a pretrained transformer that solves PU classification entirely through in-context learning. PUICL is pretrained on synthetic PU datasets generated from randomly instantiated structural causal models, exposing it to a wide range of feature-label relationships and class-prior configurations. At inference time, PUICL receives the labeled positives and the unlabeled samples as a single input and returns class probabilities for the unlabeled rows in one forward pass, with no gradient updates or per-task fitting. On 20 semi-synthetic PU benchmarks derived from the UCI Machine Learning Repository, OpenML, and scikit-learn, PUICL outperforms four standard PU learning baselines in average AUC and accuracy, and is competitive on F1-score. These results show that the in-context learning paradigm extends naturally beyond fully supervised tabular prediction to the semi-supervised PU setting.

</details>


### [652] [Variational Smoothing and Inference for SDEs from Sparse Data with Dynamic Neural Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05606)
*Yu Wang,Arnab Ganguly*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel method to infer latent trajectories and parameters in stochastic differential equations (SDEs) by learning a conditional score through neural networks, leading to efficient posterior trajectory sampling and scalable inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in calibrating SDE models from sparse, noisy data, particularly tackling path degeneracy and scalability issues in classical smoothing methods.

Method: Introduced a neural network-based approach to learn a conditional backward-in-time score using Kolmogorov backward equations. An ELBO-based variational EM-style procedure combines posterior trajectory sampling with parameter estimation.

Result: The method demonstrated accurate and stable inference on nonlinear systems with minimal observations, outperforming classical MCMC methods in scalability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances efficiency and scalability for state smoothing and parameter estimation in SDEs, showing promise for complex, partially observed systems.

Abstract: Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) provide a flexible framework for modeling temporal dynamics in partially observed systems. A central task is to calibrate such models from data, which requires inferring latent trajectories and parameters from sparse, noisy observations. Classical smoothing methods for this problem are often limited by path degeneracy and poor scalability. In this work, we developed a novel method based on characterization of the posterior SDE in terms of conditional backward-in-time score defined as the gradient of a function solving a Kolmogorov backward equation with multiplicative updates at observation times. We learn this conditional score using neural networks trained to satisfy both the governing PDE and the observation-induced jump conditions, thereby integrating continuous-time dynamics with discrete Bayesian updates. The resulting score induces a posterior SDE with the same diffusion coefficient but a modified drift, enabling efficient posterior trajectory sampling. We further derive a likelihood-based objective for learning the SDE parameters, yielding an evidence lower bound (ELBO) for joint state smoothing and parameter estimation. This leads to a variational EM-style procedure, where the neural conditional score is optimized to approximate the smoothing distribution, followed by a maximization step over the SDE parameters using samples from the induced posterior. Experiments on nonlinear systems demonstrate accurate and stable inference with a very few observations demonstrating significant improved scalability compared to classical MCMC methods.

</details>


### [653] [Spherical Flows for Sampling Categorical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05629)
*Jannis Chemseddine,Gregor Kornhardt,Gabriele Steidl*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method for learning generative models for discrete sequences in a continuous embedding space, specifically on a spherical domain, using the von Mises-Fisher (vMF) distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve generative modeling for discrete sequences by leveraging the advantages of working on a spherical domain instead of traditional Euclidean spaces or probability simplices.

Method: The authors introduce a technique that uses the von Mises-Fisher (vMF) distribution for noise processes and utilizes its closed-form conditional score. They reduce the problem to a scalar ODE and derive a bounded solution. The key object for learning is the posterior, trained via cross-entropy loss, enabling ODE and predictor-corrector sampling.

Result: The proposed vMF-based approach, combined with predictor-corrector sampling, outperforms geodesic and Euclidean methods in experiments on Sudoku and language modeling tasks.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the effectiveness of using vMF-driven generative models on spherical domains, significantly improving performance on discrete sequence generation compared to existing alternatives.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning generative models for discrete sequences in a continuous embedding space. Whereas prior approaches typically operate in Euclidean space or on the probability simplex, we instead work on the sphere $\mathbb S^{d-1}$. There the von Mises-Fisher (vMF) distribution induces a natural noise process and admits a closed-form conditional score. The conditional velocity is in general intractable. Exploiting the radial symmetry of the vMF density we reduce the continuity equation on $\mathbb S^{d-1}$ to a scalar ODE in the cosine similarity, whose unique bounded solution determines the velocity. The marginal velocity and marginal score on $(\mathbb S^{d-1})^L$ both decompose into posterior-weighted tangent sums that differ only by per-token scalar weights. This gives access to both ODE and predictor-corrector (PC) sampling. The posterior is the only learned object, trained by a cross-entropy loss. Experiments compare the vMF path against geodesic and Euclidean alternatives. The combination of vMF and PC sampling significantly improves results on Sudoku and language modeling.

</details>


### [654] [Spectral Lens: Activation and Gradient Spectra as Diagnostics of LLM Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05683)
*Andy Zeyi Liu,Elliot Paquette,John Sous*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The study explores the hidden mechanisms in language model training using spectral measurements and identifies unique findings regarding batch size, activation spectra, and predictive dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the hidden mechanics of language model training that are obscured by metrics like training loss and throughput, and to suggest operational diagnostics for better understanding.

Method: The researchers use activation covariance and per-sample gradient SVD spectra as diagnostic tools, employing a controlled family of decoder-only models based on a modded NanoGPT codebase.

Result: They discover that batch size affects representation geometry, activation covariance spectra forecast efficiency, and spectral dynamics can distinguish architectural from execution-based gains.

Conclusion: Spectral analysis provides predictive insights into model efficiency and hidden mechanics, supporting task-aligned feature learning with a mechanistic explanation.

Abstract: Training loss and throughput can hide distinct internal representation in language-model training. To examine these hidden mechanics, we use spectral measurements as practical and operational diagnostics. Using a controlled family of decoder-only models adapted from the modded NanoGPT codebase, we introduce an empirical protocol based on activation covariance and per-sample gradient SVD spectra. This dual-view reveals three empirical findings and one mechanistic explanation. First, batch size acts as a latent determinant of representation geometry: runs that reach equal loss settle into systematically distinct activation spectra. Second, the activation covariance tail measured early in training reliably forecasts downstream token efficiency. Third, movement of the activation spectrum head (leading modes), together with gradient spectra, characterizes underlying learning-dynamics changes, separating learning-side architectural improvements from primarily execution-side gains. These predictive and diagnostic signals persist across the 12-, 36-, and 48-layer model tiers. Finally, a mechanistic model proves the main observations and explains how activation covariance spectra correlate with task-aligned feature learning.

</details>


### [655] [Fourier Feature Methods for Nonlinear Causal Discovery: FFML Scoring and FFCI Testing in Mixed Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05743)
*Joseph D. Ramsey*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces two Random Fourier Feature (RFF)-based methods for scalable nonlinear causal discovery: FFML for scoring and FFCI for independence testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery using Gaussian processes and kernel methods is appealing but computationally expensive. This paper aims to create scalable techniques without compromising accuracy.

Method: FFML approximates GP marginal likelihood using RFFs, enabling efficient computation. FFCI performs fast conditional independence tests using individual variable featurization and ridge residualization.

Result: FFML performs better in causal discovery tasks compared to baselines using the BOSS framework. FFCI achieves better recall and structural improvement at reduced computational cost.

Conclusion: The proposed methods, FFML and FFCI, are effective computational tools for nonlinear causal discovery, offering scalability and competitive empirical performance.

Abstract: Gaussian process marginal likelihood scores and kernel conditional independence tests are theoretically appealing for nonlinear causal discovery but computationally prohibitive at scale. We present two complementary RFF-based methods forming a practical toolkit for score-based, constraint-based, and hybrid causal discovery.
  The Fourier Feature Marginal Likelihood (FFML) score approximates the exact GP marginal likelihood by replacing the n x n kernel Gram matrix with a finite-dimensional feature representation, reducing cost to O(nm^2 + m^3) while retaining the probabilistic interpretation and automatic complexity penalty of the exact score. FFML extends to mixed (continuous + discrete) parent sets via a product-kernel construction, with a Kronecker path for small discrete parent sets and a Hadamard-product path otherwise.
  The Fourier Feature Conditional Independence (FFCI) test is a fast nonparametric CI test for mixed data. Each variable is featurized individually: continuous variables via RFF or Orthogonal Random Features (ORF), discrete variables via a Cholesky-factored categorical feature map, with blocks concatenated. Conditioning uses ridge residualization in feature space; the test statistic is a Frobenius norm of the residualized cross-covariance, approximated as a weighted sum of chi-squared variables.
  Although FFML and FFCI share the same RFF/ORF machinery, they differ architecturally: FFML builds a joint kernel over a parent set for scoring, while FFCI featurizes variables individually for testing. We compare FFML to TRFF, a penalized Student-t regression alternative. Empirically, BOSS+FFML outperforms linear and kernel-ridge baselines on nonlinear data. When run through the same PC-Max implementation, FFCI and RCIT exhibit complementary precision-recall profiles: RCIT is more precise while FFCI achieves better recall and lower SHD, and runs in one third the time.

</details>


### [656] [Transformers Provably Implement In-Context Reinforcement Learning with Policy Improvement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05755)
*Haodong Liang,Lifeng Lai*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores the capacity of transformers to execute in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL), proving their ability to perform policy-improvement methods and establishing theoretical guarantees for their training dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand whether transformer architectures can effectively internalize and execute classical reinforcement learning algorithms in-context, addressing the challenge of learning from trajectory data without parameter updates.

Method: The paper uses explicit parameter constructions to demonstrate the implementation of RL methods with linear self-attention transformers and designs a teacher-mimicking training procedure with theoretical analysis of gradient flow dynamics.

Result: Transformers trained on tabular MDPs replicate the parameter constructions developed in the study and achieve strong in-context control performance on unseen MDPs, confirming theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: Transformers are capable of implementing reinforcement learning algorithms in-context, bridging theoretical understanding and empirical evidence in ICRL, with guarantees for training convergence and performance on new tasks.

Abstract: We investigate the ability of transformers to perform in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL), where a model must infer and execute learning algorithms from trajectory data without parameter updates. We show that a linear self-attention transformer block can provably implement policy-improvement methods, including semi-gradient SARSA and actor-critic, via explicit parameter constructions. Beyond existence, we design a teacher-mimicking training procedure, analyze its gradient-flow dynamics, and establish the first convergence guarantee in the ICRL literature: under suitable richness conditions on the training MDP distribution, gradient flow converges locally and exponentially to an optimal parameter manifold corresponding to the desired RL update. Empirically, training transformers on randomly generated tabular MDPs confirms these predictions: the learned models recover the parameter structure of our explicit constructions and, when deployed on unseen MDPs, deliver strong in-context control performance. Together, these results illuminate how transformer architectures internalize and execute classical reinforcement learning algorithms in context, bridging mechanistic understanding and training dynamics in ICRL.

</details>


### [657] [Ratio-based Loss Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05808)
*Lena Helgerth,Andreas Christmann*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper surveys ratio-based loss functions in machine learning, focusing on their general mathematical properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically explore ratio-based loss functions and enable future utilization in machine learning where relative errors matter.

Method: The study systematically investigates properties like continuity, convexity, Lipschitz-continuity, and differentiability of ratio-based loss functions.

Result: The paper provides a structured analysis of ratio-based loss functions and proposes new loss functions for future exploration.

Conclusion: Understanding the mathematical properties of ratio-based loss functions facilitates research into their application across various machine learning tasks.

Abstract: Algorithms in machine learning and AI do critically depend on at least three key components: (i) the risk function, which is the expectation of the loss function, (ii) the function space, which is often called the hypothesis space, and (iii) the set of probability measures, which are allowed for the specified algorithm. This paper gives a survey of a certain class of loss functions, which we call ratio-based. In supervised learning, margin-based loss functions for classification tasks depending on the product of the output values $y_i$ and the predictions $f(x_i)$ as well as distance-based loss functions depending on the difference of $y_i$ and $f(x_i)$ for regression are common. Distance-based loss functions are in particular useful, if an additive model assumption seems plausible, i.e. the common signal plus noise assumption. However, in the literature, several loss functions proposed for regression purposes have a multiplicative error structure in mind and pay attention to relative errors, i.e. to the ratio of $y_i$ and $f(x_i)$. In this survey article, we systematically investigate such ratio-based loss functions and propose a few new losses, which may be interesting for future research. We concentrate on investigating general properties of ratio-based loss functions like continuity, Lipschitz-continuity, convexity, and differentiability, because these properties play a central role in most machine learning algorithms. Therefore, we do not focus on some specific machine learning algorithm to derive universal consistency, learning rates, or stability results. Instead, we want to enable future research in this direction.

</details>


### [658] [CITE: Anytime-Valid Statistical Inference in LLM Self-Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05873)
*Hirofumi Ota,Naoto Iwase,Yuki Ichihara,Junpei Komiyama,Masaaki Imaizumi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces the CITE algorithm for ensuring controlled certification of answers from large language models, addressing challenges in error control and data-driven stopping rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise error control during response aggregation and data-dependent sampling in large language models is a critical challenge.

Method: The authors propose the CITE algorithm, which leverages intersection-union testing with E-processes to certify answer modes with controlled false certification rates.

Result: They prove theoretical guarantees for error control, derive stopping-time rates independent of answer set size, and demonstrate empirical gains in simulations and experiments.

Conclusion: CITE achieves reliable certification of answer modes under arbitrary stopping rules, with practical and theoretical improvements over existing approaches.

Abstract: Large language models often improve reasoning by sampling multiple outputs and aggregating their final answers, but precise and efficient control of error levels remains a challenging task. In particular, deciding when to stop sampling remains difficult when the stopping rule is data-dependent and the set of possible answers is not known in advance. We study anytime-valid certification of a prespecified target answer as the unique mode of the model's response distribution, a guarantee distinct from answer correctness. We propose the Certification by Intersection-union Testing with E-processes (CITE) algorithm, which provably controls false certification at any prescribed level under arbitrary data-driven stopping, without requiring prior knowledge of the answer category set. We also prove an category-set-size-free stopping-time rate, establish matching minimax lower bounds up to constants in the main regime, and extend the construction to confidence-weighted voting. Simulations and LLM self-consistency experiments show empirical error control and improved certification in diffuse-tail settings.

</details>


### [659] [Tuning Derivatives for Causal Fairness in Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05882)
*Filip Edström,Guilherme W. F. Barros,Tetiana Gorbach,Xavier de Luna*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework for addressing fairness in AI systems with continuous protected attributes, blending Statistical Parity (SP) and Predictive Parity (PP) principles via path-specific partial derivatives and demonstrating better performance in balancing fairness trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing bias in AI predictions, particularly for continuous protected attributes, as existing methods mostly cater to categorical attributes, leaving a gap in fairness considerations.

Method: Developing a causal fairness framework using structural causal models, integrating SP and PP principles through partial derivatives, and introducing a fair tuning algorithm to adjust predictors.

Result: The framework is validated on simulated and real data, showcasing superior performance compared to prior methods when Predictive Parity is applied.

Conclusion: The proposed fairness framework and tuning algorithm effectively balance the trade-off between SP and PP, offering a novel solution for continuous attribute fairness in AI.

Abstract: Artificial-intelligence systems are becoming ubiquitous in society, yet their predictions typically inherit biases with respect to protected attributes such as race, gender, or age. Classical fairness notions, most notably Statistical Parity (SP), demand that predictions be independent of the protected attributes, but are overly restrictive when these attributes influence mediating variables that are considered business necessities. Recent causal formulations relax SP by distinguishing allowed from not-allowed causal paths and by complementing SP with Predictive Parity (PP), requiring the predictor to replicate the legitimate influence of business-necessities. Existing path-based definitions are mainly practical when applied to categorical attributes. This paper introduces a new framework for fairness in structural causal models that is tailored to continuous protected attributes. We formalize SP and PP through path-specific partial derivatives, establish conditions under which these criteria coincide with prior causal definitions, and characterize when a fair predictor, one that satisfies SP along not-allowed paths while achieving PP along allowed paths, exists. Building on this theory, we propose a fair tuning algorithm that either constructs such a predictor or, when not possible, allows for a trade-off between SP and PP. We present experiments on simulated and real data to evaluate our proposal, compare it with previously proposed methods, and show that it performs better when PP is considered.

</details>


### [660] [Towards Reliable LLM Evaluation: Correcting the Winner's Curse in Adaptive Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05973)
*Yang Xu,Jiefu Zhang,Haixiang Sun,Zihan Zhou,Tianyu Cao,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces SIREN, a protocol to improve evaluation reliability in LLM tuning by addressing score inflation and selection bias issues through controlled inference methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for LLMs often suffer from selection-sensitivity when reused benchmark items artificially inflate observed scores and distort conclusions.

Method: SIREN protocol involves freezing post-search shortlist items, separating selection and evaluation processes, and using Gaussian multiplier bootstrap for uncertainty quantification in fixed shortlist tuning settings.

Result: Simulations and experiments demonstrate that SIREN provides stable, selection-aware inference while traditional winner-based reporting can lead to optimistic and misleading deployment insights.

Conclusion: SIREN offers a robust method to analyze tuning outcomes and ensures credible evaluation results under finite budget constraints, improving LLM benchmark reliability.

Abstract: Adaptive prompt and program search makes LLM evaluation selection-sensitive. Once benchmark items are reused inside tuning, the observed winner's score need not estimate the fresh-data performance of the full tune-then-deploy procedure. We study inference for this procedure-level target under explicit tuning budgets. We propose SIREN, a selection-aware repeated-split reporting protocol that freezes the post-search shortlist, separates splitwise selection from held-out evaluation, and uses an item-level Gaussian multiplier bootstrap for uncertainty quantification. In a fixed-shortlist regime with smooth stabilized selection, the estimator admits a first-order item-level representation, and the bootstrap yields valid simultaneous inference on a finite budget grid. This supports confidence intervals for procedure-performance curves and pre-specified equal-budget and cross-budget comparisons. Controlled simulations and MMLU-Pro tuning experiments show that winner-based reporting can be optimistic and can change deployment conclusions, while SIREN remains close to the finite-sample reporting target.

</details>


### [661] [TabCF: Distributional Control Function Estimation with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05993)
*Geping Chen,Chunlin Li,Tianzhong Yang,Zhengyuan Zhu,Jing Zhou*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces TabCF, a simple and tuning-light method for distributional causal inference using tabular foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for causal effect estimation often focus only on mean effects and require substantial tuning efforts, creating a need for streamlined tools enabling broader distributional analysis.

Method: TabCF utilizes tabular foundation models for control function regression, providing accurate and transparent estimation of causal distributional quantities, including means and quantiles, with a copula-based approximation for multivariate outcomes.

Result: TabCF demonstrates favorable performance compared to other methods in both synthetic and real-world data scenarios, proving effective across a range of datasets.

Conclusion: TabCF is valuable for practitioners due to its efficiency in distributional causal inference and may serve as a strong baseline for future research in method development.

Abstract: Instrumental variable (IV) and control function (CF) methods are powerful tools for causal effect estimation in the presence of unmeasured confounding, yet most existing approaches target only mean effects and/or demand substantial fitting and tuning effort. In this paper, we introduce a simple method, TabCF, for control function regression using tabular foundation models, which enables accurate, fast, identification-transparent, and tuning-light causal estimation of distributional quantities, such as interventional means and quantiles; we also propose a copula-based approximation for multivariate outcomes. TabCF performs favorably against representative methods across a broad range of small- to medium-sized synthetic and real data scenarios. The central message is two-fold: for practitioners, it highlights that TabCF is an effective tool for distributional causal inference; for researchers, it suggests that the proposed approach could be considered a strong baseline for future method development. Code is available at https://github.com/GepingChen/TabCF.

</details>


### [662] [Gaussian mixture models in Hilbert spaces via kernel methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05996)
*Daniel López-Montero,Antonio Álvarez-López,Marcos Matabuena*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Gaussian mixture model for infinite-dimensional data in Hilbert spaces, using kernel mean embeddings. It provides efficient optimization algorithms and validates the framework through experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address issues in modeling and clustering of infinite-dimensional, time-evolving random objects, which are complex and challenging to analyze in Hilbert spaces.

Method: The paper uses a Gaussian mixture model framework with kernel mean embeddings and develops algorithms for clustering Hilbert-space-valued data. It also includes theoretical proofs for the validity and approximation capabilities of the model.

Result: The framework is demonstrated to work well across diverse data types (e.g., $L^2$ functional data and random graphs). It is shown to accommodate complex data geometries often found in real-world applications like medicine.

Conclusion: The proposed Gaussian mixture framework is both effective and widely applicable in clustering high-dimensional, ill-structured data, offering significant potential for practical and theoretical advancements.

Abstract: Modern datasets across many disciplines increasingly consist of time-evolving, potentially infinite-dimensional random objects, such as dynamic functional data, which are naturally modeled in Hilbert spaces. In these settings, characterizing probability measures, for example, through densities, can be ill-defined or technically challenging. Motivated by clustering applications, we propose a Gaussian mixture framework for Hilbert-space-valued data based on kernel mean embeddings and develop efficient optimization algorithms for estimation. We establish theoretical guarantees showing that the proposed algorithm is well defined and that the model yields a dense class of approximations in infinite-dimensional spaces. We evaluate the framework through extensive experiments on diverse structures and data geometries, including $L^2$-functional data and random graphs in Laplacian spaces arising in modern medical applications.

</details>


### [663] [Expressivity of Bi-Lipschitz Normalizing Flows: A Score-Based Diffusion Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06172)
*Meira Iske,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper studies the expressivity and distributional approximation properties of bi-Lipschitz normalizing flows using score-based diffusion models, deriving universal approximation results and deterministic convergence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the distributional approximation capabilities and regularity constraints imposed by bi-Lipschitz normalizing flows, and to establish deterministic convergence guarantees for diffusion-based transport.

Method: The probability flow ODE of variance-preserving diffusion is used to connect score regularity with the regularity of transport maps. Score regularity is verified for a variety of target densities, enabling analysis of approximations and convergence.

Result: Universal $L^1$-density approximation among all probability densities is achieved with Gaussian pullbacks induced by bi-Lipschitz transport maps. For Gaussian convolution targets, convergence in Kullback-Leibler divergence is obtained without early stopping.

Conclusion: Bi-Lipschitz variance-preserving flows possess strong universal approximation capabilities, providing deterministic guarantees for score-based diffusion transport methods and advancing theoretical understanding of these models.

Abstract: Many normalizing flow architectures impose regularity constraints, yet their distributional approximation properties are not fully characterized. We study the expressivity of bi-Lipschitz normalizing flows through the lens of score-based diffusion models. For the probability flow ODE of a variance-preserving diffusion, Lipschitz regularity of the score induces a flow of bi-Lipschitz diffeomorphic transport maps. This ODE bridge allows us to analyze the distributional approximation power of bi-Lipschitz normalizing flows and, conversely, derive deterministic convergence guarantees for diffusion-based transport. Our key idea is to use the probability flow ODE to link regularity of the score to regularity of the induced transport maps. We verify score regularity for broad target densities, including compactly supported densities, Gaussian convolutions of compactly supported measures and finite Gaussian mixtures. We obtain a universal distributional approximation result: Gaussian pullbacks induced by bi-Lipschitz variance-preserving transport maps are $L^1$-dense among all probability densities. For Gaussian convolution targets, we further obtain convergence in Kullback-Leibler divergence without early stopping.

</details>


### [664] [When Does Trimming Help Conformal Prediction? A Retained-Law Diagnostic under Calibration Contamination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06204)
*Congye Wang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates trimming suspicious calibration points as a response to contamination in conformal prediction, focusing on its effects on clean-target coverage and proposing diagnostics and numerical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contamination in conformal prediction can compromise reliability. The motivation is to understand how trimming calibration points impacts clean-target coverage and contamination reduction.

Method: The study treats trimming as conditioning rather than purification, analyzes score-CDF transfer with finite-sample identities, derives bounds on transfer gaps, and provides diagnostic and certificate templates.

Result: Trimming aids contamination handling under certain score-separation conditions but may fail when retention probabilities are poorly managed. Numerical guarantees are offered via finite-sample audits.

Conclusion: Trimming can be effective but comes with limitations; success depends on proper anomaly score separation and retention ratio management. Diagnostic and certification tools enhance practical application.

Abstract: Trimming suspicious calibration points is a common response to contamination in conformal prediction. Its effect on clean-target coverage, however, is governed by the retained law induced by trimming, not by the contamination level alone. We analyse fixed-threshold trimming as conditioning rather than purification. It replaces the contaminated calibration law with a retained law, reducing clean-target coverage to a one-dimensional score-CDF transfer problem with an exact finite-sample identity. A componentwise bound on the transfer gap gives a population-level diagnostic. This separates a clean-side covariance cost from a retained-contamination cost, governed by the dirty-to-clean retention ratio. Trimming helps when the anomaly score separates retention probabilities while remaining score-neutral on the clean population. Otherwise, it cannot substantially reduce contamination through the retained mixture coefficient. We also give finite-sample certificate templates that provide numerical guarantees under independent audit.

</details>


### [665] [Super-Level-Set Regression: Conditional Quantiles via Volume Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06210)
*Sacha Braun,Michael I. Jordan,Francis Bach*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Super-Level-Set Regression (SLS) to directly optimize prediction regions for multivariate regression, avoiding the limitations of prior density-estimation approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges posed by traditional density-estimation methods in constructing prediction regions with conditional coverage in multivariate regression.

Method: SLS directly parameterizes and optimizes the geometric boundaries of target conditional level sets, avoiding full distribution estimation and leveraging volume-preserving frontier functions.

Result: SLS captures complex, multimodal, and disjoint conditional structures in an end-to-end approach, improving efficiency and accuracy.

Conclusion: This work shifts multivariate conditional quantile regression from density-first methods to a more flexible geometric optimization strategy using SLS.

Abstract: Constructing minimum-volume prediction regions that satisfy conditional coverage is a fundamental challenge in multivariate regression. Standard approaches rely on explicitly estimating the full conditional density and subsequently thresholding it. This two-step plug-in process is notoriously difficult, sensitive to estimation errors, and computationally expensive. One would like to instead optimize the region directly. Formulating a direct solution is challenging, however, because it requires minimizing a volume objective that is coupled with the conditional quantiles of the model's own estimation error. In this work, we address this challenge. We introduce super-level-set regression (SLS), a novel mathematical framework that successfully resolves this implicit coupling, allowing us to directly parameterize and optimize the geometric boundaries of the target conditional level sets. By bypassing full distribution estimation and leveraging flexible volume-preserving frontier functions, our approach natively captures complex, multimodal, and disjoint conditional structures end-to-end. Ultimately, SLS offers a new perspective on multivariate conditional quantile regression, replacing the restrictive assumptions of density-first methods with a direct geometric optimization strategy.

</details>


### [666] [ConquerNet: Convolution-Smoothed Quantile ReLU Neural Networks with Minimax Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06265)
*Tianpai Luo,Fangwei Wu,Weichi Wu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces ConquerNet, a method to improve deep quantile regression via convolution-smoothed quantile ReLU networks, overcoming non-smooth optimization challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantile regression is useful for understanding data distributions but optimizing it for deep models is challenging due to its non-smooth pinball loss.

Method: The authors design ConquerNet, which smooths the quantile loss via convolution while maintaining quantile properties, and provide theoretical risk bounds under Besov function classes.

Result: The approach improves both estimation accuracy and training efficiency, especially for extreme quantiles, outperforming existing quantile neural networks.

Conclusion: ConquerNet effectively addresses deep quantile regression challenges, offering smooth optimization, theoretical guarantees, and practical performance improvements.

Abstract: Quantile regression is a fundamental tool for distributional learning but poses significant optimization challenges for deep models due to the non-smoothness of the pinball loss. We propose ConquerNet, a class of \textbf{con}volution-smoothed \textbf{qu}antil\textbf{e} \textbf{R}eLU neural \textbf{net}works, which yield smooth objectives while preserving the underlying quantile structure. We establish general nonasymptotic risk bounds for ConquerNet under mild conditions, providing minimax guarantees over Besov function classes. In numerical studies, we demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms standard quantile neural networks at multiple quantile levels, showing improved estimation accuracy and training efficiency across the board, with particularly pronounced advantages at high and low quantiles.

</details>


### [667] [Multimodal Deep Generative Model for Semi-Supervised Learning under Class Imbalance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06289)
*Heegeon Yoon,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents a multimodal semi-supervised learning model that addresses class imbalance by integrating deep generative modeling and Student’s t-distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate bias caused by class imbalance in semi-supervised learning while capitalizing on the advantages of multimodal data.

Method: The model uses separate encoders for each modality, shared latent variables across modalities, adopts Student’s t-distributions instead of Gaussian distributions, and employs a $γ$-power divergence objective.

Result: Empirical experiments showed superior classification performance on benchmark and real-world datasets when compared to baseline approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed model effectively improves generalization for partially labeled multimodal data under imbalanced class conditions.

Abstract: When modeling class-imbalanced data, it is crucial to address the imbalance, as models trained on such data tend to be biased towards the majority classes. This problem is amplified under partial supervision, where pseudo-labels for unlabeled data are predicted based on imbalanced labeled data, propagating the bias. While recent semi-supervised models address class imbalance, they typically assume single-modal input data. However, with the growing availability of multimodal data, it is essential to leverage complementary modalities. In this article, we propose a multimodal deep generative model for semi-supervised learning under class imbalance. Our approach uses separate encoders for each modality, sharing latent variables across modalities, and simplifies joint posterior computation with a product-of-experts method. To further address class imbalance, we replace typical Gaussian distributions with Student's t-distributions for the prior, encoder, and decoder, better capturing the heavy-tailed latent distributions in imbalanced data. We derive a new objective function for training the proposed model on both labeled and unlabeled data using $γ$-power divergence. Empirical results on benchmark and real-world datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms baseline methods in generalization, achieving superior classification performance for partially labeled multimodal data with imbalanced class distributions.

</details>


### [668] [End-to-End Identifiable and Consistent Recurrent Switching Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06315)
*Carles Balsells-Rodas,Zhengrui Xiang,Xavier Sumba,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces $Ω$SDS, a flow-based estimator for sequential data with regime-switching dynamics, addressing identifiability and practical limitations of previous methods. It achieves better disentanglement and forecasting through exact likelihood optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges in learning identifiable representations in deep generative models for sequential data, particularly addressing restrictive assumptions and approximation limitations in existing techniques.

Method: The paper establishes theoretical identifiability for a class of recurrent nonlinear switching dynamical systems and introduces $Ω$SDS, which employs flow-based estimation and exact likelihood optimization via expectation-maximization.

Result: $Ω$SDS is empirically validated to improve disentanglement of latent structures and enhance forecasting accuracy compared to VAE-based methods.

Conclusion: The introduced approach broadens identifiability bounds and offers a practical solution with superior performance for modeling regime-switching dynamics in sequential data.

Abstract: Learning identifiable representations in deep generative models remains a fundamental challenge, particularly for sequential data with regime-switching dynamics. Existing approaches establish identifiability under restrictive assumptions, such as stationarity or limited emission models, and typically rely on variational autoencoder (VAE) estimators, which introduce approximation gaps that limit the recovery of the latent structure.
